responseSet.title: Freudin jalanjäljillä [In Freud's Footsteps] edited by Minna Juutilainen, Ari Takalo Teos, Helsinki, 2009; 326 pp; €24
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freudin jalanjäljillä [In Freud's Footsteps] edited by Minna Juutilainen, Ari TakaloTeos, Helsinki, 2009; 326 pp; €24

Anssi Perakyla

Anssi Perakyla

PO Box 54 (Unioninkatu 35), FIN-00014, Helsinki, Finland
e-mail: anssi.perakyla@helsinki.fi

Anssi Perakyla  

The task of this collection is set out in the editors' introduction (p. 15): to present the widespread psychoanalytic thinking in Finnish academia, to make known the various reflections of psychoanalytic thinking in everyday life, and to respond to some of the recent criticisms targeted at psychoanalysis in Finnish public discourse. Thus the editors take up quite a challenge: the book is to serve as an explication of some of the contemporary scholarly uses of psychoanalytic concepts and, at the same time, it is to help in popularizing psychoanalysis and in defending psychoanalysis.

The authors come both from clinical practice and from academic research; a number of them have their feet in both worlds. I should say that the authors and the editors manage rather well the triple task they have taken up. In what follows, I will assess the three tasks - to popularize, to explicate, and to defend - each in turn, starting from the task of popularizing.

The line between good popularization and skilful exposition of theoretical ideas is not very sharp: skilful exposition of ideas may involve also good popularization. While all the contributions of the book speak to non-specialist readers, some of the chapters are particularly successful in conveying core psychoanalytic ideas in a clear and accessible way. Stig Hägglund presents some of the key tenets of the theory of drive, illustrated with extracts from film and literature. Leena Klockars gives an overview of the key developmental theories in psychoanalysis, supported by short clinical vignettes. Jussi Kotkavirta, while discussing the import of Freudian ideas for moral philosophy, also explicates Freud's structural model in a succint way.

The second task of the collection is to explicate the contemporary scholarly uses of psychoanalytic theories and concepts. Most of the contributions deal with this task. Three chapters on the interface between neuroscience and psychoanalysis stand out. Ari Juutilainen sets the scene with a historical overview, starting from Freud's relation to neuroscience at different phases of his career, and ending with the current neuropsychoanalytic discussions. Thereafter, two somewhat disparate interpretations of the relation between the two fields are presented. Johannes Lehtonen emphasizes the fundamental continuum between psychoanalysis and neuroscience (as well as other sciences).

[[RunningHead]]

New Methods for Integrating Divergent Concepts (Parts 1 & 2)1111

Eike Hinze

Eike Hinze

Eike Hinze  

At the beginning of his presidency Charles Hanly launched an initiative which had as its objective the integration of psychoanalytic theories. The point of departure was the plurality of theoretical approaches in contemporary psychoanalysis which, on the one hand, could be envisaged as the expression of creative developments in a vital scientific scene. But, on the other hand, this plurality may lead to conceptual inconsistencies impeding the discourse of analysts among each other and the dialogue with other sciences. A deterioration of psychoanalytic practice may be the final consequence. The ‘New Scientific Initiative’ led to the foundation of two Project Committees, the Project Committee on Clinical Observation and the Project Committee on Conceptual Integration.

11Moderator: Werner Bohleber. Panellists: Dominique Scarfone (Canada): Sverre Varvin (Norway); Samuel Zysman (Argentina): Juan Pablo Jimenez (Chile).746

responseSet.title: Freud, the Reluctant Philosopher by Alfred I. Tauber Princeton University Press, Princeton, NJ, 2010; 318 pp; $24.95
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud, the Reluctant Philosopher by Alfred I. TauberPrinceton University Press, Princeton, NJ, 2010; 318 pp; $24.95

Elliot Jurist PHD

Elliot Jurist

138th Street and Convent Avenue
NAC 8/109, New York NY 10031, USA
e-mail: ejurist5@gmail.com

Elliot Jurist  

Many commentators offer a passing nod to Freud's interest in philosophy, dutifully noting the early influence of his teacher, Brentano, before expeditiously moving on to accept Freud's modest disclaimers of any serious investment in philosophical concerns. Alfred Tauber's Freud, the Reluctant Philosopher differs in placing Freud's relationship to philosophy on center stage, making the argument that Freud “knew philosophy and drew from that tradition as he built his theory, consciously or not” (Tauber, p. 25). Thus, we are treated to a detailed exploration of the influence of Brentano on Freud (Freud took six courses with him from 1874-76, the only nonmedical courses he took at university, and he met with him outside class [p. 29]), a series of reflections that situate Freud in relation to the dominant philosophical schools of the time (positivism, historicism and neo-Kantianism). Tauber presents the intriguing but controversial argument that Kant deserves to be recognized as the philosopher who was most crucial for Freud (his “philosophical North Star” [p. 9]), and an additional but not well-developed argument that locates Freud as part of a triangle with Kant and Nietzsche (pp. 11, 164, 173). Along the way, Tauber draws attention to a number of philosophers who are not commonly linked to Freud, such as Spinoza, Kierkegaard and Dewey, and offers insightful readings of philosophers whose work is no longer widely known, such as Dilthey, Windelband, Lange and Meynert.

245

responseSet.title: Opening Plenary
responseSet.newSectionName: Reports of Panels Held at the 47th Congress of the International Psychoanalytic Association, Mexico City, 3-6 August 2011
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Opening Plenary11

Peter Dunn

Peter Dunn

Peter Dunn  

Professor Charles Hanley convened the 47th Congress of the IPA in Mexico City. Luisa Marino welcomed participants on behalf of the IPSO. Governor Marcelo Luis Ebrard Caubon extended the good wishes of the people of Mexico City. Professor Hanley reviewed the goals of the IPA and the work of the 100th anniversary year. Steven Ellman discussed the organization and goals of the proceedings in his capacity as chair of the Scientific Program Committee. The Committee had opted for a plenary format with associated discussion groups to maximize the opportunity for the exchange of ideas. The program was designed as a group inquiry into the core concepts of sexuality, dreams and the unconscious with the aim of furthering the conceptual integration of psychoanalytic theory.

Consistent with the anniversary theme that looking back is a precondition for moving forward, the first paper illuminated the birth of psychoanalysis. Seeds of core psychoanalytic concepts by Use Grubrich-Simitis is based on the recently published letters between the lovers Sigmund Freud and Martha Bernays. The author studied the letters in search of the seeds of ideas that would later develop into the core concepts. She was able to isolate passages in which Freud described the rudiments of such concepts as sublimation and unconscious identification. In other passages Freud's comments on his own dreams indicate an awareness of the contribution of the day residue and, in one instance, the role of displacement in disguising Martha's dream image. Large sections of the letters read like an intellectual dialogue and not the love letters of a genius to his muse. Grubrich-Simitis concludes that Martha's contribution has been overlooked and her intellect underestimated.

The Opening Plenary concluded with the announcement that Bernardo Bertolucci was the recipient of this year's Award for Extraordinarily Meritorious Service to Psychoanalysis. Andrea Sabbadini accepted the award on behalf of Bertolucci and read a commentary to accompany a documentary that the director had prepared for the Congress.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Uprooted Minds: Surviving the Politics of Terror in the Americas by Nancy Caro HollanderRoutledge, New York, NY 2010; 403 pp; $36.95

Eli Zaretsky

Eli Zaretsky

Fifth Floor, 80 Fifth Avenue, New York, NY 10011, USA
e-mail: elizarestsky@earthlink.net

Eli Zaretsky  

The complicity of psychoanalysis with authoritarian political regimes is one of the signal facts in its history. The most important example is that of Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud and Ernest Jones who all sought to work with German psychoanalysis after it had been rendered Judenrein, that is, after the Jewish analysts had been expelled. Also of great significance was the cooperation of many American ego psychologists with the Cold War, even with McCarthyite repression. To be sure, the opposite is also the case. There were psychoanalysts who resisted Nazism and who stood up to Cold War bullying. Uprooted Minds is a study of both sides of this analytic conundrum, as applied to the Americas.

The latter phrase is the key to understanding the book. Although largely devoted to Latin America, Hollander's perspective places the US in the role of hegemon for the entire hemisphere. Nothing that happens in Latin America happens without US influence. Furthermore, in a brilliant turn, she takes the experiences of violence and oppression with which Latin American (especially Argentinean and Brazilian) psychoanalysis has been inflected, and turns those experiences back on the study of the United States. In particular, she uses Latin American analytic understandings of trauma, mourning and working through to examine the North American response to 9/11.

In 1991 Horacio Etchegoyen was elected President of the International Psychoanalytic Association. The election marked the recognition of the leading role that Latin Americans played in the global analytic profession. Brazilian analysis flourished, with perhaps 11 major institutes. Buenos Aires billed itself as the ‘world capital of psychoanalysis.’ When the IPA met there in 1991 it commanded a level of news coverage comparable to that which the International Chess Association commands in Moscow, or the World Series in New York.

Yet, ten years earlier, Jacques Derrida had written:

This that we would call from now on the Latin America of psychoanalysis is the only zone of the world where, confronting each other or not, a strong psychoanalytic society and a society … that practises torture on a large scale, torture that is not limited any longer to the easily identifiable classically brutal forms, coexist. This torture … sometimes utilizes psycho-symbolic techniques.

responseSet.title: On: Vesa Talvitie and Juhani Ihanus and Neuropsychoanalysis (2011)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Vesa Talvitie and Juhani Ihanus and Neuropsychoanalysis (2011)

Gilbert Pugh

Gilbert Pugh

44 Groombridge, Hackney,
London E9 7DP, UK
E-mail: g_pugh@tiscali.co.uk

Gilbert Pugh  

Dear Editor,

The article by Vesa Talvitie and Juhani Ihanus (Int J Psychoanal 92 (6), 2011) is disappointing not only because it maintains the same nihilistic stance of its predecessor, the Blass and Carmeli (2007) paper to which it claims to be an extension, but, mindsets aside, it contains factual errors and unwarranted assumptions.

Assumptions are made throughout this paper that neurological ideas are the same as neuropsychoanalytical ones and that the only unconscious processes recognized by neuropsychoanalysis are implicit and procedural. Many neuropsychoanalysts agree that these represent a non-repressed unconscious in addition, but this does not mean that neuropsychoanalysis as a whole rejects the idea of a Freudian unconscious (Mancia, 2004).

The authors surmise that “no neuropsychoanalyst has ever argued that psychoanalytic models will be reduced to the neuroscientific ones” (p. 1585).

1039

responseSet.title: On: Reply to Gilbert Pugh and Brian Johnson
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Reply to Gilbert Pugh and Brian Johnson

Vesa Talvitie and Juhani Ihanus

Juhani Ihanus

PO Box 9, Helsinki, FI-00014 Finland
E-mail: vesa.talvitie@alumni.helsinki.fi, juhani.ihanus@helsinki.fi

Juhani Ihanus  

Dear Editor,

In our article, we argued that at the core of neuropsychoanalysis there is a conflict between psychoanalytic and mainstream (neuro)science conceptions. If neuropsychoanalysis adopted the mainstream view of neuroscience, i.e. if it resigned the ‘cornerstone’ (the Freudian mental unconscious), psychoanalysts would probably to a large extent lose interest in neuropsychoanalysis. In our article, we explicated the consequences of the other alternative: in so far as neuropsychoanalysis leans on the Freudian idea of a causally efficacious unconscious that does not become reduced to neurophysiological matters, there is a serious disagreement with the views of mainstream neuroscience. In this issue, neuropsychoanalysis cannot avoid taking sides: it either leans on the Freudian conception or does not.

From the scientific viewpoint it is not crucial to dispute about what polls say about how popular neuroscience is among psychoanalysts, what Freud (who has recently been appointed even a co-founder of neuropsychoanalysis) said a century ago, whether or not one (Blass and Carmeli, Talvitie and Ihanus) is positive enough towards the endeavour of neuropsychoanalysis, and whether one has read the right neuropsychoanalytic texts.

In his letter, Dr Gilbert Pugh starts with heavy labelling rhetoric (“the nihilistic stance”, “factual errors”, “unwarranted assumptions”), positioning us altogether against creative interdisciplinary endeavours. Perhaps through our mirror neuron systems, we were disappointed to see that our arguments for the need to focus on the core conflict and openly explicate the premises of neuropsychoanalysis were thus evaded and displaced.

Apart from particular psychoanalytic topics, the interests of neuropsychoanalysis are tied to the general question concerning the relation between psychology and neuroscience (or psychological and neurobiological explanations). Each viewpoint has its scope and limits, and outside psychoanalysis we find lively debates around this issue (for example, Bennett et al., 2007; Kendler and Parnas, 2008; Schouten and Looren de Jong, 2007). Neuropsychoanalysis, however, seems to possess an unquestionable agenda of promoting interdisciplinarity, and people taking up the issue of the relation between psychology and neuroscience are easily labelled lukewarm (as Dr Pugh does here).

[[RunningHead]]

Until the Fires Stopped Burning: 9/11 and New York City in the Words and Experiences of Survivors and Witnesses by Charles StrozierColumbia University Press, New York, NY, 2011; 312 pp; $26.95

Kevin Kelly MD

Kevin Kelly

9 Metrotech Center, Brooklyn, NY, 11201, USA
E-mail: kevinvkellymd@gmail.com

Kevin Kelly  

The author of this idiosyncratic, thought-provoking volume describes himself as both an historian and a psychoanalyst, and therein lies a methodological challenge. The process of psychoanalysis involves the exploration of personal history, but the two fields differ in ways that are important for the project of studying the psychological effects of an historical event. The methods and assumptions of clinical psychoanalysis, which entails the study of an individual life over an extended period of time, are not easily adapted to the study of multiple lives over a brief period. As the book's title implies, the author defines a period of study sharply, from the morning of September 11, 2001 until the following December 20th, when the Fire Department of New York (FDNY) declared the fires at the World Trade Center (WTC) site officially extinguished.

The author's strategy for meeting this challenge borrows both from the psychoanalytic tradition, in relying primarily on anecdotes for illustration and evidence, and from historiographic practice, in comparing and contrasting individuals' reactions to experiences that are similar but not identical. These efforts are only as convincing as anecdotal evidence can ever be, but the comparisons made are creative and the results often provocative.

1079

Tietoisuuden ruumiillisuus. Mieli, aivot ja olemassaolon tunne [The Embodiment of Consciousness: The Mind, the Brain and the Sense of Existence]: by Johannes Lehtonen; Duodecim, Porvoo, 2011; 2007 pp; €30,50

Ilpo Lahti

Ilpo Lahti

Uudenmaankatu 11 C, 20500 Turku, Finland E-mail: ilpo.lahti@finnet.fi

Ilpo Lahti  

When listening to a patient and experiencing the work on the psyche that he or she is doing as an individual and we are doing together, what is actually happening? Is there something involved besides our brains that cannot simply be reduced to the operation of the nervous system? What is the mind, the conscious or the unconscious, and what is their origin? With advancements in cognitive brain research, new answers have been found to these eternal questions about the relationship of mind and body, mind and brain; however, these answers give rise to new questions, thus generating new constructs.

In psychoanalytic circles, there is also increased interest in this problem, which Freud, in his own time, touched upon before turning his attention primarily to consciousness and the development of the structure of personality.

1524

responseSet.title: On: Skype and Privacy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Skype and Privacy

John Churcher

John Churcher

4 Rippingham Road, Withington, Manchester M20 3EX, UK
E-mail: churcher@aulos.co.uk

John Churcher  

Dear Editor,

Jill Savege Scharff's recent paper discusses modifications of the psychoanalytic setting (or frame) using the telephone and/or Internet to allow sessions to take place when analyst and patient are in different geographical locations (Scharff, 2012). Her paper has the merit that it addresses both technological and psychoanalytical aspects of the setting, and the relations between them. However, there are inaccuracies in her account of the technology, including VoIP1 telephony of which Skype is one example, and these could have implications for psychoanalytic practice. Her paper raises many interesting and important issues but my comments here are confined to her assumptions about security and privacy. She writes:

VOIP has the advantage that it is a secure method of communication … VOIP is thought to be secure because it sends information in fragmented bundles that do not connect until they reach the destination. But because it does not offer encryption, VOIP is not as secure as using a Videotechnology Company (VTC).2

(Scharff, 2012, p. 82)

This is not entirely clear or accurate and could be misleading. The phrase “fragmented bundles” apparently refers to packet-switching, which is the basis of all Internet communication, including Internet-based VoIP such as Skype, but also email, web browsing, etc. Packet-switching without encryption offers only limited security against physical interception, and none against eavesdropping techniques that use readily available software. Some VoIP and web-based videoconferencing services do not use encryption, but others do. Skype calls are encrypted, which may partly explain the widely held but unwarranted belief that Skype is secure.

Because Skype uses proprietary software its encryption method is not publicly available and therefore cannot be independently evaluated. Moreover, recent empirical research (Dupasquier et al., 2011) has shown that significant information is recoverable from intercepted Skype voice calls, without decryption, using statistical properties of the encrypted data.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Corps malade et adolescence [The ailing body and adolescence] by Irina Adomnicai1 Translated by Sophie LeightonÉditions in Press, Paris, 2004; 220 pp; €19

Christine Jean-Strochlic

Christine Jean-Strochlic

133 BD Montparnasse, 75006 Paris, France
e-mail: christinejean-strochlic@wanadoo.fr

Christine Jean-Strochlic  

Irina Adomnicai has been working at the Centre Psychosomatique Adultes et Enfants (IPSO) in Paris for many years and this book has emerged

1Translated by Sophie Leighton251

directly from her clinical experience there. Its constant interweaving of clinical practice and theory, each drawing on the other in turn, deserves special mention. This book also won its author the P. Male prize.

Before turning to the text, it is worth considering Cathérine Chabert's preface, which indicates some research in relation to severe somatic illnesses in adolescents that was conducted from “psychoanalytic perspectives both in theoretical, metapsychological terms and in clinical, therapeutic terms” (p. 7). Highlighting the rigour of a method with a consistent epistemological foundation, Adomnicai emphasizes the permanent dialectic between the psychic treatment and its requirements and the attention accorded to the somatic problem. This also highlights the idea of somatic illness as a counter-cathexis of the sexual impulses in an upheaval of infantile dynamics. This impulse is often accompanied by a difficulty in the separation/individuation process.

Adomnicai's hypothesis consists in the idea that somatic illnesses are illnesses of the ego, which enables her to consider adolescent somatizations as a counter-cathexis of the sexual impulses involved in the mobilization imposed by the adolescent process on the psyche. She regards taking account of the ailing body as a current theoretico-clinical necessity and she immediately draws us into her project, presenting an adolescent problematic that fails through the unexpected onset of a somatic illness. In other words, Adomnicai invites us into:

The psychic clinical treatment of the somatic symptom in adolescence through a hypothesis of somatic illness as a de-signified signifier, somatic illness as a screen illness in relation to sexuality and, finally, the anti-nostalgic quality devolved to somatic illness by adolescents' familial and individual psychic apparatus confronted with the elaborative establishment of puberty.

responseSet.title: Seduction, Receptivity and the ‘Feminine’ in Peter Greenaway's The Pillow Book
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Seduction, Receptivity and the ‘Feminine’ in Peter Greenaway's The Pillow Book

Nicholas Ray

Nicholas Ray

Leeds LS2 9JT, UK — n.j.ray@leeds.ac.uk

Nicholas Ray  

As is well known to anyone familiar with Peter Greenaway's cinema, systems abound in his work. It is packed with organizational devices whose purpose is to impose order on existence: structural patterns, taxonomies, representational regimes, meta-narratives and so on. Such systems may operate diegetically, in the preoccupations and obsessions of individual characters, or non-diegetically, punctuating the very composition of a film. Yet they are habitually shown to be unequal to the reality it is their raison d'ětre to organize; comically or tragically, they end up dwarfed or burst apart by the multiplicities they exist to contain, blindly outflanked by what is. As such, Greenaway's cinema follows a vector running militantly counter to much mainstream - and certainly Hollywood - film. Whereas the latter tends to unfold in the direction of resolution and clarification, moving narratorially from confusion to stability, from fragmentation to synthesis, Greenaway repeatedly takes the yearning for synthesis and resolution as an explicit and ironic focus. Pre-emptively dramatizing the endeavour to create organizing principles, myths and narratives, his films insistently trace their etiolation and dissolution, their inevitable failure to ‘stav[e] off chaos’.1

As several commentators have pointed out, the articulation of this entropic vision is frequently coded in gendered terms.2 Greenaway himself has observed that: “Film after film after film” expresses his fundamental “mistrust […] of [the figure of] the male hero” (Willoquet-Maricondi, 2008, p. 309), and this emerges most emphatically in the repeated affiliation between his male protagonists and the organizing codes and systems that are progressively undermined in the course of each work. It is Greenaway's men who ordinarily fabulate and/or impose these codes, hubristically seeking to master existence by interpreting, cataloguing, regulating it. They rarely survive longer than the organizing matrices they sponsor. Greenaway's women, on the other hand, do tend to survive and are regularly affiliated with the unmasterable or uncircumscribable excess on which the artificial codes and systems of containment founder.

responseSet.title: On: Maurizio Balsamo, the Analyst at Work - Sabina
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Maurizio Balsamo, the Analyst at Work - Sabina

Matthias von der Tann

Matthias von der Tann

8 Elsworthy Terrace,
London NW3 3DR, UK
E-mail: mvondertann@gmail.com

Matthias von der Tann  

Dear Editor,

The difference in Sabina's internal situation between the described earlier and later phases of her analysis with Maurizio Balsamo (2011) denotes without doubt a significant progress in a very demanding process for both partners of the analytic couple.

I would like to examine this progress from a specific point of view. It seems apparent that by way of the analysis the internal object of the twofold absent deadening mother has been able to be transformed from an overwhelming presence into a significant but not domineering element (characterized by the representation as bystander in the dream).

I would like to use this opportunity as a preliminary communication to introduce a concept which, according to my knowledge, has not been described before. The progress described above can be conceptualized as the transformation of an internal object into an ‘internal subject’. This term, ‘internal subject’, is meant to characterize the result of a metamorphosis, a very significant development of an internal object. Insofar as internal objects and the relationships between the self and those objects can only be differentiated conceptually but not empirically, the transformation of an internal object cannot be differentiated from a transformation of the relationship to the internal object.

This change, however - if and insofar it happens - goes along with several changes which cannot be described here for reasons of space. Essential would be what could be called a ‘subjectivization’ of this structure (usually conceptualized as ‘internal object’), turning it more into a unit being equipped with qualities and capacities similar to a human subject, thereby becoming an ‘internal subject’. One important quality of this internal structure is the degree to which it provides a unit usable for the self (‘use of the object’) which can respond and therefore can be addressed and communicated with.

This transformation is well visible in the development of the internal (internalized) mother-object described in the earlier stage of Sabina's analysis. We witness the emergence of an ‘internal part-subject’ (the mother who is interested, but in books and not in the analysand), which is enacted by the analyst suddenly remembering [Einfall] this aspect of the mother and making use of this in his interpretation.

responseSet.title: The Shadow of the Tsunami and the Growth of the Relational Mind: by Philip M. Bromberg; Routledge, New York and London, 2011; 215 pp; $39.95
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Shadow of the Tsunami and the Growth of the Relational Mind: by Philip M. Bromberg; Routledge, New York and London, 2011; 215 pp; $39.95

Richard Zimmer MD

Richard Zimmer

11 East 80th Street, New York, NY 10075, USA E-mail: rbz1@columbia.edu

Richard Zimmer  

Philip Bromberg's work has been celebrated within certain sectors of the psychoanalytic community and underappreciated in others. In The Shadow of the Tsunami and the Growth of the Relational Mind, Bromberg's central clinical focus, as in his two previous books, Standing in the Spaces (1998) and Awakening the Dreamer (2006), is trauma; but Bromberg does more than focus on a theoretical and technical approach to a narrow group of patients. Rather, he uses the concept of trauma as an entry point to re-imagine aspects of psychic structure in all patients, and to reconceptualize how the treatment situation works, not so much as a means of resolution of intrapsychic conflict, but as a facilitator for healing splits in the self, and of emotional growth through the expansion of the capacity to tolerate, articulate, and process previously unformulated affective experience.

Although, in this volume, Bromberg adds further clarity to the theoretical model of trauma and dissociation put forward in his first two books, the major theoretical innovation in The Shadow of the Tsunami and the Growth of the Relational Mind is Bromberg's effort to integrate the findings of modern neuroscience with his relational theory of normal and pathological psychic development and of the mechanism of action of psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. The book begins with a 37-page Foreword by the neuropsychoanalyst Allan Schore.

1519

[[RunningHead]]

Tormenti di Anime, Passioni, Sintomi, Sogni [Torments of the Soul: Passions, Symptoms, Dreams]1: by Antonino Ferro; Raffaello Cortina, Milano, 2010; 216 pp; €21.00

Giovanni Foresti

Giovanni Foresti

Corso Garibali 40, 27100 Pavia, Italy; E-mail: giforest@tin.it

Giovanni Foresti  

The title of this book published by Antonino Ferro in 2010 paraphrases a famous film by Georg Wilhelm Pabst, Secrets of a Soul [Geheimnisse einer Seele], which reached movie theatres in 1926. Availing himself of consultations with Karl Abraham and Hans Sachs, Pabst succeeded in a difficult undertaking that was in some ways paradoxical: to make known the method and goals of psychoanalysis by utilizing an art form that was at that time still silent. The film is thus a brilliant paradox: “a silent movie about the talking cure” (Sabbadini, 1994). Making use of Bion's thinking, Ferro pursues an objective no less arduous and equally paradoxical: to contribute to the development of psychoanalysis, emphasizing not what it has been able to explain up until now, but instead discussing the limits of our knowledge of the psychic apparatus.

In the first chapter of the book, the author clearly explicates the conceptual perspective that seems most convincing to him - the only one, in fact, that he finds reasonable. Psychoanalytic theories, he writes, are conceptual systems that are barely comparable to each other, in part because they have been constructed in thinking about various aspects of experience (the Newtonian mechanics of tangible objects is one, while the microphysics of particles is another), and in part because their utilization is influenced by defensive phenomena analogous to keloids (overgrown scars on our will to know) and to para-amnesias (confabulations and pseudo-memories that have come into being in order not to recognize lapses in memory).

Ferro maintains that, in order to avoid having to tolerate the limited degree of our knowledge, we tend to become “like shipbuilders who insert plugs into the keel of a ship”: “patches to avoid sinking into not knowing” (Ferro, p. 6).2 Given that we do not like to face our ignorance and that uncertainty terrifies us, we often function like a religious community that sanctifies its theoretical canon (orthodoxy) and defends it, excommunicating thoughts and thinkers who seem to be at odds with “real psychoanalysis.” When things go particularly badly, “the operation is completed in sessions through ‘transformations in hallucinosis’” suffered by patients (when “we project what we have constructed, thought, or more often learned from books, and then we read this ‘projection’ as evidence” (Sabbadini, 1994, p. 7).

[[RunningHead]]

100 Years of the IPA: The Centenary History of the International Psychoanalytical Association 1910-2010: Evolution and Change: edited by Peter Loewenberg, Nellie L. Thompson; International Psychoanalytic Association/Karnac, London, 2011; xxvii + 560 pp. $28.95

Aaron Esman MD

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street, New York, NY, 10028, USA E-mail: ahesman@earthlink.net

Aaron Esman  

The years following Sigmund Freud's historic 1909 visit to the United States were momentous ones for the evolution of psychoanalysis. At the Nuremberg Congress in 1910, with Freud in attendance, the International Psychoanalytical Association was established while the next year a devoted group of his followers formed the New York Psychoanalytic Institute. The same year saw the birth of the American Psychoanalytic Association. Thus 2010-11 provided the occasion for multiple centennial celebrations, mixing reminiscence with serious reflections on the current state of the field and its prospects for the future.

The present volume is a substantial product of this commemorative activity. Its editors, distinguished both as psychoanalysts and historians, have assembled a wide-ranging group of essays that give testimony to the global expansion of psychoanalysis since those formative years. At the same time, these essays illuminate both the similarities and the variations that have evolved in patterns of administration, training and practice in response to adaptation to cultural, intellectual and interpersonal differences across nations and continents.

A massive tome of some 500 pages, the book includes reports - some brief, some extended and scholarly - from some 41 of the 93 Constituent Societies, Study Groups and Allied Centers that comprise the Association, together with statements by certain of its officers, particularly its Presidents since 1969. Clearly, neither space nor time makes it possible to condense all this material into a manageable review; accordingly, I shall attempt to highlight certain themes that emerge from this voluminous text.

Growth

When it was founded in 1910, the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA) included some 200 members. As it approached its Centenary, the Association boasted some 12,000 members of all categories. This increase is due to a variety of factors, first among them the spread of interest in Freud's work among literate populations in the Western world in the years preceding and following World War I.

responseSet.title: Farewell
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Farewell

Robert Michels MD

Robert Michels

e-mail: ijpnaeb@gmail.com

Robert Michels  

My term as Joint Editor-in-Chief of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis will end in February 2012. The experience has been challenging and frustrating at times, but it has also been exciting and rewarding.

From the beginning of my tenure I was very much aware that I was following in the footsteps of giants. Many of them I never knew, starting with the first, Ernest Jones; there were several I did know and admired - Joe Sandler, David Tuckett, Paul Williams, and Glen Gabbard. I had never met my joint editor Dana Birksted-Breen before our term started; we had to learn how to work together and to do so while working 3500 miles apart. Moreover, as we assumed the editorship, the British Society was negotiating with commercial publishers to take over many of the administrative aspects of the Journal, a process that required major transitions and that led to the current arrangement with Wiley-Blackwell.

Fortunately, we were able first to retain and then to recruit a spectacular group of Regional Editors. We started with Antonino Ferro and Elias da Rocha Barros, and then we recruited Catalina Bronstein, Jay Greenberg, Ricardo Bernardi, Sergio Lewkowicz, Jean-Michel Quinodoz, Jorge Canestri and Lucy LaFarge. They have made our work a special pleasure. We also value the experience of working with other members of our Editorial Board, a dedicated group of analysts who devote a great deal of time to the work required to uphold the Journal's high standards.

Ultimately, the quality of any journal is dependent on the quality of the submissions it receives; they are the life-blood of a journal, even though we are only able to publish a minority of the papers we review. At the International Journal it has been my honor to work with the many authors who contribute their professional work. Some of these authors enter into a continuing dialogue with the Editors as their work is refined and improved on its way to publication. A much larger number of authors have their submissions reviewed but then rejected; their continued interest and support of the Journal and their willingness to submit new articles are essential to our project of sustaining the quality of the Journal. I see our authors, along with our readers, as the major constituencies of the Journal.

responseSet.title: On: Psychoanalysis and Contemporary Psychology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Psychoanalysis and Contemporary Psychology

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs

New York 11530, USA E-mail: josephs@adelphi.edu

Lawrence Josephs  

Dear Editor,

Wallerstein (2012) provides an excellent portrayal of the history of psychoanalysis as it appears from the perspective of someone situated within American Psychiatry and the American Psychoanalytic Association. As a psychology professor, analytically trained outside of the American Psychoanalytic Association, I can provide a somewhat different perspective of the challenge that contemporary scientific psychology poses to psychoanalysis as a theory of mind and as a method of treatment.

Wallerstein (2012) provided this characterization of the two psychological paradigms with which psychoanalysis competes: “The first is the learning-theory and the stimulus-response model … and the behavior modification technologies derived from it” (p. 384-385) and “the so-called third force, the existential-phenomenological tradition” (p. 385). This characterization was certainly valid in the 1970s when I was an undergraduate psychology major. At that time, psychoanalysis was the only depth psychology that studied unconscious processes, emotional and personality development, childhood trauma, defense mechanisms, sexual fantasy life, etc. It was easy to characterize the competition as superficial, as behavioral psychology didn't address human subjectivity and existential-phenomenological approaches addressed human subjectivity but not unconscious processes. As a consequence, psychoanalysis could easily attract bright and introspective students, as the case study method seemed better suited to studying these “deeper” issues than paper and pencil self-report measures or conditioning experiments with rats and pigeons.

Times have changed and the emerging paradigm in scientific psychology is looking at the traditional sub-disciplines of academic psychology (i.e. cognitive, social, personality, & developmental psychology as well as motivation and emotion) from an evolutionary perspective that attempts to systematically establish the adaptive mental mechanisms that characterize the human mind (See Buss, 2011). Psychological topics that were once the exclusive province of psychoanalytic depth psychology have given rise to systematic programs of empirical research that study the adaptive unconscious, the evolution of sexual desire, the righteous mind, the dynamics of romantic love, and self-deception. Experimental techniques, like mindset priming, now exist for activating unconscious thoughts and emotions and seeing how such activation affects conscious attitudes as well as behavior so that systematic exploration of unconscious processes is no longer dependent on the case study method.

Unusual Interventions: Alterations of the Frame, Method, and Relationship in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: edited by Salman Akhtar; Karnac, London, 2011; 229 pp; £22.99

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305, Chicago, IL 60603, USA E-mail: docaig@aol.com

Arnold Goldberg  

I had an imaginary conversation with the editor of this book about his very felicitous choice of a title. Inasmuch as Dr. Akhtar is a poet as well as a psychoanalyst, it should be no surprise that he would veto my own immediate thought that the book is about ‘deviations’ from the norm in favor of the much more appropriate ‘unusual interventions.’ It does take a certain effort for someone who has been taught that there is a right way to do things to realize that the very words ‘right’ and ‘wrong’ have locked us into a straitjacket of normativity (another of my favorite words).

This is a book that asks the reader to suspend propriety about the conduct of treatment and also to open one's mind about a host of taken-for-granted issues in psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. It starts, after an introductory list of the actions of some of the great names in psychoanalysis (such as Freud feeding the Rat Man)

Immaginando [Imagining] by Domenico Chianese, Andreina FontanaFranco Angeli, Milan, 2010; 237 pp; €29

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

Via Cardano 77, Pavia I-27100, Italy
E-mail: ninoferro3@aol.com

Antonino Ferro  

One does not often come across a book that brings together philosophy, the history of ideas, psychoanalysis and the world of art. Yet that is the complex task that the two authors of this book have managed to accomplish. To use a metaphor from the book, let us get on board the pick-up truck that the boy in the first chapter of this unusual book somehow ‘sees’ among the countless stains on the floor, for thereby we can observe the precarious conquest of the symbols that will enable us to embark on equally unimaginable journeys. We enter the caves of Altamira, where bison crouching in various positions come to life in ways that open up new horizons. But what is the status of images in philosophy? Or better, what has it been? From its humble origins, it has increasingly become an object of interest and study, even if for Freud “thought concerning images is primitive thought […] In dreams we speak through images, only by necessity […] images must be transformed into words” (p. 31).

This is a book that, as it develops, presents us with a very rich iconography, opening up new worlds in the extraordinary and in-depth encounters it allows us to make with characters and scenarios dear to us analysts.

An example of this is the fascinating story that unfolds in the chapter ‘Sopravvivenza e Formule del Pathos’ [‘Survival and Formulae of Pathos’], where we enjoy a magical encounter with Binswanger at the Bellevue, with Freud, and with Warburg while he delivered his lecture of 1923 entitled ‘The Ritual of the Snake’. Warburg here discusses the power of images to wound and to heal, the conquest of symbols but at the same time the precariousness of such a conquest. In this way, he denies that culture could cure human suffering. We are not far here from Freud's Civilization and Its Discontents (1930).

For reasons of space, I can do no more than mention in passing the other powerful pages dedicated to Warburg, to his illness, to his recovery, to his works, and to his complex relationship with Binswanger and Kraepelin, which are recounted in very interesting documentary sources. In the same way, I cannot dwell on the other ‘doubles’ we come across in the book: Nietzsche and Burckhardt, Freud and Fliess, and in a sense Bion and Beckett too - all of whom are figures who also belong to our mental life, taking on a three-dimensional quality and becoming real people in the hands of the authors.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Publisher's Apology

The International Journal of Psychoanalysis Volume 93, Number 3

Due to an error during the production process, issue number 3 was erroneously assigned page numbers starting on page 271, which is the same start page number used for issue 2. The issue was published in print and online before the error was spotted, using these incorrect citation details.

Following consultation with the Editor, Dana Birksted-Breen, and colleagues at the Institute of Psychoanalysis and Wiley, it has been decided to correct the publication details for issue 3.

We have therefore undertaken to amend the page numbers for all the published articles in issue 3, and replace the online version of each article, as well as reprinting and redistributing all copies of issue 3 to subscribers.

On the following page we reproduce the full Table of Contents for issue 3, showing the corrected page numbers (and indicating the original incorrect page numbers, which have been struck-through).

When citing any articles from Issue 3, please use the corrected pagination details as shown in the table of contents attached. For instance, for the first article in the issue, please use:

Abbasi A (2012).‘A very dangerous conversation’: The patient's internal conflicts elaborated through the use of ethnic and religious differences between analyst and patient. Int J Psychoanal 93: 515-34. doi: 10.1111/j.1745-8315.2012.00601.x

Instead of the erroneous

Abbasi A (2012).‘A very dangerous conversation’: The patient's internal conflicts elaborated through the use of ethnic and religious differences between analyst and patient. Int J Psychoanal 93: 271-90. doi: 10.1111/j.1745-8315.2012.00601.x

The Publisher apologises to the authors, readers, and subscribers to The International Journal of Psychoanalysis for this mistake, and for creating any confusion regarding citation to the articles in this issue.

1099

[[RunningHead]]

Illusions et désillusions du travail psychanalytique [Illusions and Disillusions in Psychoanalytic Work] by André GreenOdile Jacob, Paris, 2010; 288 pp; €23

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

3 rue des Lyonnais 75005 Paris, France
e-mail: dominique@bourdin.biz

Dominique Bourdin  

One characteristic feature of this book, which is André Green's most recent publication, is the courage with which he confronts not only the difficulties that arise in psychoanalytic work but also the disillusions that it brings in its wake. Based on more than 50 years' work as a psychoanalyst, this book brings together a whole sector of his experience - that of disappointing outcomes - and combines clinical observations from Green's own work and that of his colleagues with his thoughts on the difficulties that have arisen. Although the cases that he explores cover a whole range of topics, his thinking is both homogenous and coherent. The examples that he gives bear witness not only to the potentialities and limits of contemporary psychoanalytic therapy but also to the depth of mental pain and to the internal resistance that stands in the way of any positive outcome in psychoanalytic work. In this book, Green explores the issues that Freud raised in his final published papers (Freud, 1937c, p. 209; 1937d, p. 257; 1940[1938], p. 141) concerning negative therapeutic reactions and interminable analyses in which a negativizing regression occurs; in such situations, the patient's psychical structure feeds into his or her malaise and appears to hold on to it rather than fight against it (Green, p. 81).

The first chapter of the book focuses on Marilyn Monroe, echoing Michel Schneider's novel on her final sessions (Schneider, 2006) after some 30 months of analysis with Ralph Greenson. By offering her a kind of adoptive home, Greenson only worsened Marilyn's pain at having been deprived of such a haven in her early life. As she herself put it, she had never belonged to anything other than fear; she is a significant example of the failure of psychoanalytic treatment, overwhelmed as she was by the catastrophic impact of fate and of the always inadequate love of her husbands, her lovers and her friends. She had all the hallmarks of a sacrificial victim who, after having aroused love without anyone ever being able to pay the price, nowadays awakens our compassion.

[[RunningHead]]

Tragic Knots in Psychoanalysis: New Papers on Psychoanalysis by Roy SchaferKarnac, London, 2009; 181 pp; £19.99

Gerald Fogel

Gerald Fogel

2455 Marshall Street, Suite 5, Portland, OR 97210, USA
e-mail: geraldfogel@comcast.net

Gerald Fogel  

Roy Schafer's authorial voice - distinctive, endlessly striving for conceptual clarity and a systematic approach - is one of the great authentic psychoanalytic voices of our time. His characteristic literary style, his passion and his precision have been present throughout his writings and Tragic Knots in Psychoanalysis is no exception. This latest addition to his vast body of work collects Schafer's papers from the past decade or so. The book is organized into three major parts or sections: On basic concepts, The internal world of conflict and phantasy, and Changing conceptions of the analytic relationship. The chapters are edited and organized with novelistic skill into a coherent whole, supplemented here and there by previously unpublished chapters that contribute to the project. There is a preface that looks at the work from the perspective of the history of psychoanalytic ideas, with special attention paid to the evolution of the author's thinking. The closing chapter of the book is entitled: ‘You Can Get There From Here’. It is a thoughtful intellectual memoir describing the author's journey in psychoanalysis throughout the years, and adds an appealing personal coda to the work.

In that personal memoir, Schafer tells us that he sees throughout all his work two “red threads.” First is his deliberate investigation of the nature of interpretation. Second, seen only in retrospect, is a “conflict between a powerful wish to accept, master, and if possible enhance received wisdom and an equally powerful inclination to challenge that heritage and find it wanting” (p. 159). In this book, he repeatedly points out this process of refashioning one's self-narrative.

The task of summarizing Schafer's complex thought is made easier by his ability to summarize himself.

488

[[RunningHead]]

Samlade Skrifter av Sigmund Freud, Index and Register [Collected Works of Sigmund Freud, Index and Register]Natur och Kultur, Stockholm, 1996-2011; 12 vols

Gudrun Bodin

Gudrun Bodin

Københavnsvej 51B, DK-3400 Hillerød, Denmark
E-mail: gudrun-bodin@mail.dk

Gudrun Bodin  

The publication of Volume XII is the completion of the new Swedish translation of the Collected Works of Sigmund Freud. It is an impressive achievement by the former Swedish Psychoanalytical Society [Svenska Psykoanalytiske Föreningen] and the former Swedish Association for Holistic

1063

Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy [Svenska Föreningen för Holistisk Psykoanalys och Psykoterapi], who united in 2010 to form the Swedish Psychoanalytical Association.

An editing committee consisting of members from both groups has been working for 16 years on the publication of the 12 volumes. The aim of this new translation of Freud has been to create a translation closer to Freud's own German language. The existing translations of Freud into Swedish have all have all had their origins in Strachey's English translation. The criticism of this translation has been that the essence of Freud's rich formulations has been watered down, and that a more scientific technical language has been created which does not correspond to Freud's original.

Paikin (1998) reviewed the first four volumes in this Journal, and found the ambitious project a successful one so far. The Swedish language was close to the original German, and the learned introductions and commenting notes put the texts into perspective for new readers and experts on Freud alike. In addition, Paikin commented on something that rarely warrants comment in a review of scientific publications: namely that the layout, design, paper quality and binding of these publications were superb. These comments are just as relevant for the final volume XII, which contains introductory papers (55 pages), a collocation of quotations (87 pages), the whole register for Volumes I-XI (180 pages) and bibliographies (190 pages).

The introduction to Volume XII is written by Hans Reiland, who shares the editors' thinking of including in the final volume, as well as the register, details of Swedish connections and relations to Freud and to psychoanalysis - in other words, how Freud's thinking and observations have been received by Swedish culture. The picture on the front page shows a postcard written personally by Freud in 1918 to a psychoanalytically interested Swedish nerve specialist ‘Herrn Dr Jacob Billström’. The postcard is kept in the archive of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Association and is the only extant piece of mail in Sweden written by Freud. Reiland suggests that this single postcard with four hand-written lines could be viewed as a symbol of the bond between Freud and Sweden. Bearing in mind that Freud was a busy writer of letters, this single postcard with four hand-written lines could be experienced as a metaphor of a thin relationship that Freud had with Sweden. Against this background the new translation of Freud's texts is proof of a relationship which has developed enormously. The Swedish Collected Works of Sigmund Freud show that Freud's thinking has gained a solid footing within a scholarly and vitally psychoanalytical group in Sweden.

responseSet.title: Response to “Minding our Metaphysics”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to “Minding our Metaphysics”11

Vesa Talvitie and Juhani Ihanus

Juhani Ihanus

PO Box 9, University of Helsinki, FI-00014 Helsinki, Finland
E-mail: vesa.talvitie@alumni.helsinki.fi, juhani.ihanus@helsinki.fi

Juhani Ihanus  

Dr Scarfone claims that we have “neglected some important problems” and committed “important category errors”. However, when he refers to the first two of these “category errors” it seems to us that he is merely arguing in favour of the view we criticized in our article: that there is an entity, domain, or sphere (the mental/psychoanalytic unconscious) that does not appear in our consciousness, and cannot be studied with the help of the scientists' equipment. If it is “a mistake” and “a waste of time” to “look for any parallelism between a non-existent or trivial ‘neural unconscious’ [or rather just the brain] and the unconscious investigated by psychoanalysis”, as Scarfone argues, then psychoanalysts need to explain how the mental unconscious is able to cause disorders such as the trembling of hands. That is, we are left wondering how, to use the example we referred to, Scarfone considers that little Hans' mental unconscious actually affects the functioning of the neurons of little Hans' brain, and/or makes his hands tremble. Moreover, to call the neural unconscious “non-existent” or “trivial” does not help to foster a dialogue between psychoanalysis and neuroscience, as we hope to do.

As we explained in our paper, we believe that regardless of how the mind is defined, no branch of psychotherapy or psychoanalysis can help making the (philosophically problematic) presupposition that the mind possesses causal power over the body/brain. We were also cautious in thinking about psychoanalysis as an autonomous discipline. It is difficult to argue that psychoanalysis is a wholly different affair than all other disciplines or psychotherapies. There is a danger that such a line of thinking would actually lead psychoanalysis to an isolated position.

We welcome the increasing study of the extended social and cultural brain and its relational aspects (relations to other bodies, other brains, and other people), but at the same time we ask how this new research is related to psychoanalysis and its renewal (from the solely intrapsychic to the intersubjective or trans-subjective). “Neurological” research, focused on the disorders and dysfunctions of the nervous system, and larger neuroscientific research on all aspects of the nervous system, can contribute to this renewal.

responseSet.authorMast: Maurice Corcos,  Philippe Jeammet,  Alexandre Morel,  Catherine Chabert and  Aline Cohen De Lara

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Current Developments in the Practice of Individual Psychoanalytic Psychodrama in France

Maurice Corcos , Philippe Jeammet , Alexandre Morel , Catherine Chabert and Aline De Lara

Aline De Lara

99 avenue Jean-Baptiste Clément, F93430 Villetaneuse, France — aline.cohendelara@orange.fr

Aline De Lara  

psychoanalysis, psychodrama, symbolization, portrayal process, play, differentiation

(Final version accepted 9 September 2011)

The authors present the history of individual psychoanalytic psychodrama and its current developments as practised in France. They put forward the technique, objectives and rules, along with the indications, limits and risks that ensue from the specific nature of this therapeutic approach. Through its technical adjustments, individual psychoanalytic psychodrama provides a therapeutic option that is appropriate to the defences prevalent in many patients that cause classical psychotherapies to fail: massive inhibition, operative functioning far removed from affects or in false self mode; phobias, disavowal or splitting of the internal psychic life and emotions; prevalence of short discharge circuits in acted-out behaviours and bodily or visceral complaints and expressions. Psychodrama utilizes these defences not in order to eliminate them but to ‘subvert’ them so that they can continue to carry out their protective role, in particular ensuring narcissistic continuity. At the same time, psychodrama relaxes these defences and facilitates a possible filtering through of the repressed material. Through the number of actors and the diffraction of transference that this allows, psychodrama provides a possibility of adjusting the potentially traumatic effect of the encounter with the object and the instigation of the transference in the regressive dimension induced by any psychotherapeutic process.

responseSet.title: Fresh Old News from Ferenczi about the Function of Dreams: The Dream as a Kur, as a Treatment and as a Gyógyászat
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fresh Old News from Ferenczi about the Function of Dreams: The Dream as a Kur, as a Treatment and as a Gyógyászat

Eugênio Dal Molin

Eugênio Dal Molin

Rua Oscar Freire 1513, apto. 92, São Paulo 05409-010, Brazil - eecdm@yahoo.com.br

Eugênio Dal Molin  

dreams, metapsychology, history of psychoanalysis, reconstruction

(Final version accepted 01 March 2012)

This article discusses a text on the function of dreams and their relation to trauma. Ferenczi intended to present this material as a talk at the 12th International Congress of Psychoanalysis, which was to take place in Interlaken, Switzerland the same year that he wrote it (1931). The entire conference, however, was postponed, and parts of this communication's content appeared in other texts in which Ferenczi rethinks the concept of trauma and its clinical significance. In the present article the author makes use of the Freud/Ferenczi correspondence to contextualize Freud's Hungarian follower's originality regarding his theorizations about different aspects of the function of dreams. In the 1931 speech, as well as in this article, Ferenczi used a patient's dream work as a clinical example of a process in which traumatic experiences and unmastered sensory impressions can be repeated to achieve a better working-through for the dreamer. The process Ferenczi describes resembles an effort of self-treatment, of self-Kur.

responseSet.title: Delusional Development in Child Autism at the Onset of Puberty: Vicissitudes of Psychic Dimensionality Between Disintegration and Development
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Delusional Development in Child Autism at the Onset of Puberty: Vicissitudes of Psychic Dimensionality Between Disintegration and Development

Francesco Bisagni

Francesco Bisagni

Via Baldissera 2, Milan 20129, Italy — francesco.bisagni@aliapsyche.it

Francesco Bisagni  

child analysis, child autism, metapsychology, psychosis

(Final version accepted 26 January 2012)

Although the psychogenetic hypotheses on child autism have been superseded, psychoanalysis can still reflect on the relational exchange and its sensory aspects in concomitance with the mental development of these patients. Without making generalizations as regards the pathogenesis, but considering the specific features of each autistic child, it may be possible to achieve an integration of those islands of competence that make up these patients' limited personal heritage. Such integration may be reached through the analysis of representational, emotional and relational transformations. The first part of this article describes the case of an autistic child in treatment from the age of four on a four-times-weekly basis who, during puberty, developed severe formal thought disorders together with delusional and hallucinatory formations. The second part develops some post-Jungian theoretical contributions, such as the concept of self as nothingness and the idea of the unsaturated archetype, so as to evaluate the function of some a-priori concepts in support of the analyst's position. These concepts are considered in relation to Bion's model of transformation, and to the formulations on dimensional awareness, especially on the shift from a two-dimensionality to three-dimensionality view, as well as to the rhythm of the object's presence and absence.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen  

I am honoured to have been appointed as editor-in-chief for a further five years and wish to thank Robert Michels for the interesting time we have had jointly editing the International Journal over the last five years. Our differing views have enhanced creative thinking. I hope and believe that this divergence has contributed to an interesting journal. I also want to thank the outgoing regional editors: Catalina Bronstein for her important contribution to the London Board, which has been the board dealing with the greatest number of papers (papers submitted in the English language not originating from the Americas); Ricardo Bernardi who, besides his editorial work, has been very active in arranging meetings in Latin America to promote a dialogue between The International Journal and the Latin American community; and Sergio Lewkowicz who, as deputy editor for Latin America has also been active in this development.

The International Journal of Psychoanalysis is unique in its aim and editorial composition. Being ‘international’ in my view means publishing the best papers from all traditions. Psychoanalysis has developed in different places along different traditions. Strong traditions are to be valued and fostered. Enabling the creative and focused pursuit of specific ways of thinking and of particular topics of interest is important for our discipline. Difference is to be respected and can also nurture cross-fertilization. Looking at divergences and convergences is important and the International Journal is also the place of publication for those who seriously wish to engage with other points of view. However, it is not necessarily the same authors who can write important papers from within their tradition as those who can engage in comparative psychoanalysis. The International Journal needs to foster both.

At this time at which our discipline is being eroded by cultural and economic factors, it is also important that the International Journal engage in a serious discussion of clinical issues as a central concern. Research findings and dialogue with other disciplines are also important in keeping the wider community on board.

[[RunningHead]]

Sentire con gli occhi. Note sull' osservazione del bambino in psicoanalisi tra formazione, clinica e ricerca [Feeling through the Eyes: Infant Observation in Psychoanalysis between Training, Clinical Practice and Research] by Valentina BoursierFranco Angeli, Milan, 2010; 191 pp; €23

Massimiliano Sommantico

Massimiliano Sommantico

via S. Caterina da Siena 39, 80132 Naples, Italy
E-mail: sommanti@unina.it

Massimiliano Sommantico  

Valentina Boursier's volume, Sentire con gli occhi. Note sull'osservazione del bambino in psicoanalisi tra formazione, clinica e ricerca [Feeling through the Eyes: Infant Observation in Psychoanalysis between Training, Clinical Practice and Research], invites us right from the outset to trace back the history and development of the psychoanalytic practice of infant observation.

259

The first part of the book sets out to examine Freud's early observations of his grandson's wooden reel game (Freud, 1920) and Winnicott's ‘observation in a set situation’ (Winnicott, 1941), moving on subsequently to survey other landmark contributions to the field, including works by Anna Freud (1950), Esther Bick (1964) and Melanie Klein (1952). Concurrently, it also provides an assessment of the rich and diverse theoretical-clinical developments of infant observation in Italy, where this practice boasts a long-standing tradition (e.g. Bonaminio and Di Renzo, 1977; Borgogno, 1978; Brutti and Scotti, 1993; Cresti et al., 2001; Nunziante Cesàro, 2003; Scotti, 2002).

In her analysis of the vast amount of works on the subject, the author highlights a number of key points of direct observation theory, which, “though seemingly already a much-studied theme, has recently gained the spotlight within a heated and complex debate that focuses with a modern approach on issues to some extent already known” (p. 13). These include the debate on the (by now well-known) representations of the ‘reconstructed child’ and the ‘observed child’, as well as considerations on the understanding of the notion of ‘fact’ in psychoanalysis.

As far as the latter is concerned, Bezoari's (2006) argument that: “The nature of clinical facts in psychoanalysis is such that the analyst can be a privileged observer in as much as he is also a subject taking part in the process of the unfolding of the events together with the patient” (p. 24) seems particularly significant and attuned to the perspective adopted in this book. Indeed, it appears to be on the same wavelength as Boursier's remarks throughout the volume regarding the attention that should be placed on the observer's subjectivity.

responseSet.title: A Commentary on ‘A Very Dangerous Conversation’ by Aisha A
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Commentary on ‘A Very Dangerous Conversation’ by Aisha A11

Laura de Posadas

Laura de Posadas

Full Member, Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association
E-mail: lauraver@adinet.com.uy

Laura de Posadas  

Introduction

It was mainly curiosity which led me to accept Dominique Scarfone's kind invitation to discuss a clinical presentation by a North American colleague. This curiosity grew when I considered the analyst's name and Pakistani origins and the fact that she was treating a Jewish patient. As Latin American and Uruguayan, I was thus being introduced into a complex cultural network so characteristic of today's world of reduced distances, intensified confrontations, and potentially devastating natural disasters.

Regarding the subtitle proposed by the author, ‘the use of ethnic and religious differences between analyst and patient’ (my emphasis), I would say that it presents to us, as do the bibliographical references, with one of Aisha's topics of interest, perhaps one of those ‘fundamental questions’ (Piera Aulagnier) which orientate the direction of each analyst's research, his/her recurrent themes and the theories we think we choose but which, in fact, choose us. Since for each analyst those ‘fundamental questions’ are not the result of conscious choice, but rather conditioned by her personal and analytic history, by the residues, traces and unconscious imprints which are the product of diverse transferential crossings-over (with one's own analyst, with supervisors, teachers and writers of our discipline and others). The effects of these are formative and have an impact on the way we approach our patients and also on our lapses and blind spots.

I will come back to my impression of the differences between patient and analyst which from my point of view had most impact on the arduous work with Mr F, which Aisha here generously shares with us, her colleagues, in a spirit of great honesty.

In terms of the title, I would also say that every analysis is always a ‘dangerous conversation’. If it were not, it would not be an analysis; it would lack that element of risk which is synonymous with the mobilization of the transference. This is a mobilization which is not limited to the patient, given that the patient also sets the analyst's transference in motion, along the lines of the day's residues, if we are receptive to these, and given that we are in a state of free-floating attention which renders us at once permeable to what we are experiencing with the patient in the session and to our own personal and collective mythologies. This peculiar quality of the analytic encounter makes it difficult to separate the ‘internal’ from the ‘external’, as I will try to show with regard to the material.

responseSet.title: Reflections on Psychoanalytic Technique with Adolescents Today: Pseudo-Pseudomaturity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Psychoanalytic Technique with Adolescents Today: Pseudo-Pseudomaturity

Viviane Mondrzak

Viviane Mondrzak

Rua Teixeira Soares 28, apt. 401, Porto Alegre 90440-140, Brazil — mondrzak@terra.com.br

Viviane Mondrzak  

adolescence, psychoanalytic technique, interpretation

(Final version accepted 28 November 2011)

The starting point for this paper is current observation of adolescents who seem unable to break the latency structure, making it difficult for the adolescence process to become established. These youngsters present with a specific set of characteristics which the author proposes to call ‘pseudo-pseudomaturity’: they seem for the most part well adapted, with an absence of unconscious conflicts. However, they differ from Meltzer's description of pseudomaturity in that the omnipotent attitude of dependence-denying is not seen. On the contrary, they seem eager to take the opportunity to have the infantile true self accepted and contained before they can safely enter the process of adolescence, with all its turbulence. Some aspects of our culture are discussed in relation to the psychic configuration described. Using fragments from the analysis of a 19 year-old patient, the paper looks at technical issues raised by these cases. There is an emphasis on the analyst's own mental processes and the importance of being able to contain the emotional turbulence that cannot be sensed by the patient. The author sets out the different modalities suggested/tested/proposed in the analytic relationship in support of the transferential work. Some questions regarding how and when to make interpretations are also discussed. In these types of cases, the psychoanalytic process carries a two-fold responsibility — to the patient and to society as a whole, in view of the creative potential that adolescents represent, essential for social change and growth.

On Not Being Able to Paint (1950) and The Hands of the Living God (1969) by Marion MilnerInternational Universities Press, Madison, CT, 1950, 1969

Jan Abram

Jan Abram

E-mail: janabram@blueyonder.co.uk

Jan Abram  

Clearly the great difficulty in thinking logically … is due to the fact that we are trying to talk about a process which stops being that process as soon as we talk about …

(Milner, 1950, p. 161)

Marion Milner (1900-1998) started writing these two exceptional and inspiring books during the early 1940s although they were published 19 years apart. They are complementary, but distinctly different accounts of overlapping inner journeys. To my mind, like the whole of Milner's work, they go to the very heart of psychoanalysis and what it means to be a psychoanalyst. Through the simplicity of her prose Milner's genius is to convey an imaginative process of Self reflection that leads to Self revelation. This takes the reader imperceptibly into the depths of her process and invokes a parallel personal process of realization. There is a quality of generosity in her writing - her discoveries feel like gifts to be treasured and carefully stored. They are exquisite by-products of the process rather than the goal of each book. Milner's implicit aim, throughout all her writings, is to explore the meaning of surrender in psychoanalysis - a process of surrender to the unconscious. Milner's authenticity in her work exemplifies the thought of a natural analyst.

Marion Milner qualified as a psychoanalyst with the Institute of Psychoanalysis in 1943 and became a Training Analyst in the 1950s. Like many analysts she worked well into her 80s and was a committed Member of the Institute of Psychoanalysis - ‘warts and all’ - as she wrote in her dedication to the New Library collection of her psychoanalytic papers, The suppressed madness of sane men (1987). She was a contemporary and close colleague of Donald Winnicott and her work is as original and as radical. Their ideas profoundly influenced each other. Sadly, until now at least, Milner has not received the full credit her work deserves. Hopefully, with these newly produced publications, the value of her important contribution to psychoanalysis will begin to be realized more comprehensively.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impact and Influence of Relational Psychoanalysis77

Phoebe Cirio

Phoebe Cirio

Phoebe Cirio  

Shelley Doctors described the relational ‘turn’ in psychoanalysis as a broad umbrella beneath which are many different theories and concepts. Stephen Mitchell, who founded the Relational School, proceeded from the American Interpersonal School and British Object Relations. There the drive-defense model of the mind was distinguished from those models that posited that the mind was formed through relationships, hence the term ‘relational theories.’ Robert Stolorow and George Atwood, collaborating with Bernard Brandchaft, created Intersubjectivity theory. They were influenced by Kohut's Self Psychology and a desire to disencumber clinical psychoanalysis from its metapsychology. There the child's mind is conceptualized as forming through interaction with, and under the influence of, the already patterned mind of significant others. Intersubjectivity is a relational theory because psychological growth and transformation are understood to occur through interaction with others, whose modes of organizing their experiences both differ from, and are similar to, one's own. All psychological products, including “fantasies, defenses or interpretations, are understood to bear the imprint of the context in which they were formed.” Citing Jessica Benjamin, Doctors noted that the goal of the relationalists is to explicate the pervasive effects of human relationships on psychological development, and on internal psychic mechanisms and fantasies.

Relational treatment is a process of ‘participatory inquiry’ and mutual influence, which seeks to understand the principles by which the patient lives. Relationalists conceive of the clinical situation as including two active parties who co-construct a psychoanalytic encounter between them. Lachmann and Beebe's observation of mothers and infants are used to describe ‘structures of interaction.’ Interaction involves self-regulation, which arises from within the individual, and mutual or interactive regulation between individuals. These two types of interaction are complimentary, coordinated and simultaneously organized. All psychological experience emerges from an intersubjective “field or system in which it is formed or transformed.” The ‘relational sensibility’ works in experience-near ways and involves a process of participatory inquiry of the complex, reverberatory system in which all people are embedded.

[[RunningHead]]

Toward Mutual Recognition: Relational Psychoanalysis and the Christian Narrative by Marie T. HoffmanRoutledge, New York, NY, 2010; 278 pp; £ 22.99

Stanley Leavy MD

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Drive, Hamden, CT 06517, USA
e-mail: stanleavy@sbcglobal.net

Stanley Leavy  

This book represents the author's attempt to combine in a single volume several subjects, each of which merits close consideration, but whose bearing on one another varies between the intimate and the hypothetical. Marie T. Hoffman, a graduate of the Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis of New York University, has undertaken a study in which the quite fascinating history of a psychoanalytic psychotherapy is interrupted by long intervals of psychoanalytic theory and history, theology, metaphysics, biography, neuroscience. The history serves as a model to illustrate points drawn from literally hundreds of quotations extracted for the purpose from as many authorities, as well as to demonstrate Dr. Hoffman's therapeutic method and her personal experience of the treatment.

Psychoanalytic studies from an explicitly Christian perspective have been unusual. Freud's aggressive atheism, taken together with his criticism of traditional sexual morality, set the stage for defensive operations on the part of religious minds. In long retrospect, the polemic carried on over psychoanalysis looks embarrassingly superficial. I recall a conversation in the 1940s with a young Catholic clinical psychologist who shyly informed me that he had just been granted clerical dispensation to read Freud's own writings, not only works about him that bore the imprimatur. Expurgation on the psychoanalytic side has mainly taken the form of ignoring the relevance of religion to the conduct of life, except as a repressive force, or, in a liberal concession, as sublimation faute de mieux. One of the merits of this very mixed bag of a book is the author's tracing of a continuous trend outside the mainline of psychoanalysis that has been not only sympathetic to Judaism and Christianity, but in a measure inspired and theoretically influenced by them.

To take hold of Dr. Hoffman's diffuse argument it might be best to start by reading the final chapter, which summarizes the converging theoretical approaches of object relations, interpersonal analysis and self psychology, as affected by Christian influences. Although she pays tribute to many well-known figures in the field, pride of place goes to Sándor Ferenczi, himself of Jewish origin, to whom the penultimate chapter of the book entitled ‘In Grateful Memory’ is dedicated. It is also one of the many instances in which an excessive ballast of background information - here in unwieldy comment on the history and character of Hungarian religion and culture - detracts from the argument. Readers whose analytic education has been conventional may be surprised to learn of the contribution of the Christian philosopher Kierkegaard, and of the Jewish philosopher Martin Buber to the thought of Ferenczi and others.
