Nancy McWilliams

Nancy McWilliams

9 Mine Street, Flemington, NJ 08822, USA
e-mail: nancymcw@aol.com

Nancy McWilliams  

Here are two new and worthy books for beginning psychoanalysts. Both Sheldon Bach and Vamik Volkan speak personally to their readers and come through as generous, supportive mentors to those trying to develop as analysts. Both authors assert the value of a psychoanalytic approach to a wide range of patients. Both offer wisdom that can only come from decades in the clinical trenches, and both provide vivid case material illustrating the topics they address. Finally, both have integrated concepts from several psychoanalytic orientations; neither claims to be the representative of a particular school.

In many ways, however, these books could not be more different: Bach's How-To Book is a deliberately small, slim paperback, while Volkan's 8.5 inches by 11.5 inches volume runs to over 300 pages. More substantially, the two authors have contrasting approaches to orienting newcomers to the field.

Psychoanalysis and Motivational Systems: a New Look by JD Lichtenberg, FM Lachmann, JL FosshageRoutledge, New York, 2010; 136 pp; £31.95

Fredric Busch

Fredric Busch

10 East 78th Street, New York, NY 10075, USA
E-mail: fnb80@aol.com

Fredric Busch  

Just as I was questioning the utility of the motivational systems approach described by Lichtenberg and co-authors (e.g. why do we need to consider an exploratory assertive system; why not just consider how conflicts about

1070

anger and autonomy could inhibit normal assertiveness?), an incident occurred that convinced me of its value. A patient brought her 9-month-old son into her session, stating that her sitter had canceled. While considering the implications of this acting in, or the enactment in my readily accepting the baby in the office, I became fascinated by his exploratory behavior. He proceeded to examine each quadrant of the room in a systematic manner, testing out the items in each section (space heater, lamp, bookshelf, clock, buzzer). He looked at me intermittently, sometimes with a smile of apparent pride in his motility, and sometimes, particularly as he approached my territory, apparently scanning for clues about my response. I considered the possibilities of the interactions that might occur under such circumstances with caregivers, from overly judgmental to overly lax. I thought about how this exploration, in part based on the response of the caregiver, might combine with the ‘aversive system’, a negative association of frustration with exploration and assertion, or how it could later link to the ‘sensual/sexual system’, becoming a source of motivation and potential conflict.

Reading the book reminded me of when as an adolescent, during an obsessive preoccupation with chess, I came across Nimzovich's Chess Praxis (1936). The theories and strategies were unconventional, creative and surprising - a whole new way of looking at the board. I immediately grasped the implications of some of them, while others yielded to intense study, and still others remained inscrutable. The fresh perspective of motivational systems theory will allow analysts to expand their range of approaches and think in new ways about the therapeutic encounter. The ‘new look’ of the title refers to a reconceptualization of Lichtenberg's original description of his theories in the highly regarded Psychoanalysis and Motivation (1989). Clinical experience and ongoing discussions led to modifications of the model that include a more intersubjective perspective, the addition of affiliative and caregiving motivational systems, a better recognition of the interplay amongst motivational systems, and the use of fractal theory to understand continuity-of-self states.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hannah Arendt, Evil and the Eradication of Thought

Coline Covington

Coline Covington

Coline Covington, 193 Brecknock Road, London N19 5AB, UK - ccovington@freemind.co.uk

Coline Covington  

evil, Hannah Arendt, dissociation, follower, nobody, person, totalitarianism, eradication of thought, ego ideal

(Final version accepted 14 September 2012)

How fit he is to sway/That can so well obey.

(Andrew Marvell, An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland 1650)

Evil deeds may be committed intentionally or out of madness, but it is those who follow orders that present us with the most complex moral, philosophical and psychological questions. In writing about the banality of evil, Hannah Arendt argues that “in granting pardon, it is the person and not the crime that is forgiven; in rootless evil there is no person left whom one could ever forgive.” Arendt postulates that “being a person” necessarily entails the acts of memory and thought. This paper explores Arendt's ideas on memory and thought and how these processes can become subverted in the service of a higher order. Clinical material illustrates Whitmer's idea of dissociation as an “impairment of subjectivity” as distinct from Freud's view of dissociation as a form of repression. This shift in theoretical perspective sheds new light on our understanding of the totalitarian state of mind, i.e. of the mind of a “nobody”, and the conditions within which evil is committed.

The Analyzing Situation by Jean-Luc Donnet (Andrew Weller, translator) Karnac, London, 2009, Psychoanalytic Ideas and Applications Series; 199 pp; £22.99

Rosine Perelberg

Rosine Perelberg

35 Hodford Road, London NW11 8NL
e-mail: rperelberg@perelberg.com

Rosine Perelberg  

This book makes the work of one of the most inspiring contemporary French psychoanalysts1 accessible to the Anglo-Saxon readership. It revisits and expands some of the themes explored in his previous publications. In this review I can only address some of them.

Method/Technique

The first chapter of the book constituted one of the three previously published texts which were introduced at the 2001 IPA Congress in Nice on the theme ‘Psychoanalysis, Method and Applications’. In this seminal and much-celebrated article, Donnet points out the inherent contradiction between the use that psychoanalysts make of the notion of method, linked to the specificity of unconscious psychic processes, on the one hand, and the requirement that analysts from different traditions have of applicable prescriptions. This is discussed later in the book in terms of his distinction between the analytic method and psychoanalytic techniques, and the tension between subjectivity and objectivity.

A fundamental paradox persists in psychoanalysis:

Any attempt to define the analytic method is faced with the contrast between what the term method suggests in the way of controlled organization, and the renunciation of control implied by free association. This paradox is necessary if the unconscious was to open to rational investigation.

(p. 21)

This leads Donnet to indicate the contradiction between the necessary, acquired knowledge on the one hand, and the need to suspend knowledge on the other. Concepts of ‘learned ignorance’ (Lacan) and negative capacity (Bion) are evoked (Chapter 2), as well as Winnicott's distinction between game and play, i.e. between a game with rules and play without rules (p. 6). Donnet quotes from Michael Parsons, who identifies the function of “guardian of play in psychoanalysis” that requires an internal activity on the part of the analyst. Donnet suggests that this refers to the superego register.

1Jean-Luc Donnet was Vice Director-Practitioner then Director Practitioner of The Centre Jean Favreau, the psychoanalytical consultation and treatment centre of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. His publications include L'enfant de ça (with André Green), a ground-breaking book that introduced the notion of blank psychosis, a precursor to his work on the negative, Le Divan Bien Tempéré, Le Surmoi Freudien and La Règie Fondamentale. Amongst his many activities in the SPP he has organized for many years the Colloquium de Deauville. A Colloquium in St Malo has been planned for September 2011, centred on the understanding of his work, and especially of this book.

responseSet.title: Commentary on José Bleger's Theory and practice in psychoanalysis: psychoanalytic praxis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on José Bleger's Theory and practice in psychoanalysis: psychoanalytic praxis

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, New York, NY-10024, USA - jaygreenberg275@aol.com

Jay Greenberg  

In his sweeping, visionary overview of the relationship between psychoanalytic theory and practice, José Bleger (2012) finds “contradictions” that have impeded the development of both. His paper, more manifesto than reasoned argument, is uncanny in its anticipation of the most important controversies that have preoccupied analytic thinkers working in all geographic regions and within all theoretical traditions since its original publication in 1969. Even given this shared landscape, however, Bleger's perspective differs in subtle and not-so-subtle ways from that of his Anglophone contemporaries. His article bears the stamp of the unique vision that characterizes psychoanalysis in the Rio de la Plata, a vision that influenced his thinking and one to which he contributed significantly.

Referring to his project as a “diagnosis” of the state of the field, Bleger calls into question many of the central assumptions that gave shape to the psychoanalysis of his time: the proper language for conducting our discourse, the ‘one-person’ model of clinical process, the nature of the analyst's objectivity, the therapeutic value of historical reconstruction, the understanding of causality, the central role accorded to sexuality, and the weaknesses of psychoanalytic organizations and their training programs. He is a passionate diagnostician: in his view, psychoanalysis suffers profoundly under the weight of its theory. As he puts it, “just as neurosis is invariably a phenomenon of human alienation, theory has been structured in a way that reflects in its own structure the same alienation” (p. 997).

This is a relatively short article, and Bleger's ideas are asserted rather than developed; he does not place them in a historical context, nor does he engage alternative perspectives or acknowledge others who were trying to develop similar points of view. But despite what the reader might infer from the article's tone, Bleger's ideas are part of a larger movement that was under way in Buenos Aires and Montevideo at the time he was writing. Enrique Pichon-Riviere (Bleger's analyst) had laid much of the foundation on which Bleger built, and Heinrich Racker, Willy Baranger and Madeleine Baranger - Bleger's contemporaries - were exploring similar terrain at around the time he was writing. More surprisingly, in light of the lack of communication among analysts working in different parts of the world, in other regions theorists with backgrounds in very different traditions were grappling with very similar problems.

responseSet.title: “On Winnicott's Clinical Innovations in the Analysis of Adults”: Introduction to a Controversy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“On Winnicott's Clinical Innovations in the Analysis of Adults”: Introduction to a Controversy

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

Heythrop College, 23 Kensington Square, London W8 5HN, UK - r.blass@ucl.ac.uk

Rachel Blass  

Contemporary psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic psychotherapists who align themselves with Winnicott's thinking all adopt many of the new theoretical concepts that he introduced and his novel conceptualisations of the ways in which the environment and the maternal object impact upon early child development. The true and false self, the transitional object and space, primary maternal preoccupation, the good-enough mother, the use of the object, play, impingement and the illusion of omnipotence are the basic building blocks of almost any Winnicottian framework. These concepts and conceptualizations are commonly applied not only to the understanding of the developmental process, but also to the understanding of the clinical one.

Despite this shared conceptual framework, there are some significant differences in the ways in which Winnicott is read and made use of. In particular, those who rely on Winnicott's work appear to differ in the degree to which they believe his contributions constitute a revision of psychoanalytic thinking and practice. It would seem that these differences become most pronounced when it comes to Winnicott's contributions to analytic practice with adults.

At one end of the spectrum, many regard Winnicott as a particularly sensitive analyst working within conventional analytic frameworks and developing them; although he elaborates, modifies, corrects and innovates, his theorizing and practice are thought to remain within the mainstream of the analytic thinking of Freud and Klein. From this perspective, Winnicott's contribution to psychoanalysis is relatively limited, and classical analysts can readily make use of his central clinical innovations without giving up their fundamental analytic stance.

In contrast to this view of Winnicott as essentially traditional, we find at the other end of the spectrum those who consider his approach to be radical and revolutionary. In particular, his innovations are thought to lead us to a wholly new approach to the therapeutic process. Winnicott himself has maintained that analysts may, at times, have to put aside psychoanalytic practice—defined by him as verbal interpretation of the transference—to practice “something else that we deem to be appropriate to the occasion” (Winnicott, 1962, p. 170). “And”, he added, “why not?” (Winnicott, 1962). From this perspective, Winnicott's contribution takes us beyond the very boundaries of the psychoanalytic.

responseSet.title: Constructions in Psychoanalysis: On the ‘Assured Conviction of the Truth of a Construction’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Constructions in Psychoanalysis: On the ‘Assured Conviction of the Truth of a Construction’

Helmut Hinz

Helmut Hinz

Gartenstraße 26, D-72074 Tübingen, Germany - helmuthinz@live.com

Helmut Hinz  

Psychoanalysis as Constructivism

What Freud referred to as “a matter for later enquiry” (1937d, p. 266), or according to H. Doolittle as “the basis for a very weighty philosophy” (1956, p. 49), can with hindsight be seen as a premonition of constructivism already inherent in psychoanalysis. Loch greatly contributed to this further research, as Eickhoff (1996) points out, in proving how “what appears to be an incomplete substitute should nevertheless produce a complete result” (Freud, 1937d, p. 265) “by extending Freud's understanding of construction, which remained true to a correspondence theory of truth, and, among other things, by giving up the distinction made between reconstruction and construction” (Eickhoff, 1996, p. 68). Loch emphasizes: “Phantasies and memories do not represent the discovery of a truth of historical character but are rather attempts to create a meaning in order for life to continue” (Loch, 1976, p. 885). He explains why in the collapse of all values it is only the mind (inner reality) that remains, synonymous with meaning: that is, firstly, as the result of the third aetiology, i.e. the Nachträglichkeit, the après-coup character of the formation of the neurosis (a memory trace is charged after the event and only becomes pathogenetic in this process); secondly, as a result of re-transcription at least three or more times (Freud, 1896, 06.12.1896), i.e. transcription into three different kinds of sign; thirdly, because the attribution of meaning to memories necessarily depends on context; and, fourthly, because language does not produce an exact 1:1 copy of experience. Alongside these I should like to draw attention to further factors that show how “making out” (Freud, 1937d, SE 23, p. 259), interpreting and constructing were already synonyms for Freud and how he found construction “by far the more appropriate description” (Freud, 1937d, p. 261). This means that psychoanalysis as the process of interpretation in the relation between analyst and analysand is constructivist. By constructivism I understand a heterogeneous number of theoretical approaches from a variety of disciplines (biology, neurophysiology, cybernetics, psychology) which share the following assumption: “The perception of the world - in all its variety - is the result of inner processes” (Baraldi et al., 1998, p. 100), that is the result of processes in the nerve cells, in organisms, in the psychic apparatus, in phantasy systems. Several further factors and psychoanalytic concepts which are central to analytic theory and practice have the character of constructions and are thus various forms of construction: for example, “inherited schemata” (Freud, SE 17, p. 119), “primal phantasies” (SE 16, p. 371)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role and Importance of Interpretation in the Talking Cure

François Sirois

François Sirois

Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, 1020, Bougainville, Quebec City, Quebec G1S 3A8, Canada - sirois.rondeau@sympatico.ca

François Sirois  

interpretation, transference, history of psychoanalysis

(Final version accepted 3 Jan 2012)

Starting with an exploration of how the concept of interpretation in analytical treatment has evolved, the author goes on to discuss the role and importance of interpretation in the changes that psychoanalysis brings about. Although interpretation is looked upon as the key element in psychoanalytic activity, the fact that it is subsumed within the transference raises questions as to its influence in the analytical domain. After discussing the foundations of interpretation with respect to the theory of psychoanalytic treatment and examining Strachey's views on this, the author defines the conditions and constraints surrounding interpretation and preparatory interventions in order to outline the essential nature of the interpretative process as seen against the wider background of the analyst's activity as manifested through speech. This leads to a discussion of the relative influence of insight and suggestion in bringing about therapeutic change. The author draws the conclusion that interpretation works as a metaphor in lifting repression.

responseSet.title: On: Minding our Metaphysics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Minding our Metaphysics

Dominique Scarfone MD

Dominique Scarfone

2983 Avenue de Soissons, Montreal, QC, H3S 1W1, Canada
E-mail: dominique.scarfone@ umontreal.ca

Dominique Scarfone  

Dear Editor,

Talvitie and Ihanus (2011) are right to posit and then to question the metaphysical assumptions behind neuropsychoanalysis, or for that matter any other scientific enterprise (p. 1588), and they correctly state that: “as far as psychoanalytic explanations refer to the mental unconscious, they cannot be verified with the help of neuroscience” (p. 1583, Abstract). But it seems to me that they have neglected some important problems common to all the conceptions that came under their scrutiny, including the ‘interfield conception’ they advocate.

Like Blass and Carmeli (2007), I believe that psychoanalysis is an autonomous discipline, endowed with a specific method and an object of study of its own, and that it would be misguided to look in other fields, such as the neurosciences, for ‘proof’ of any of its tenets. However, I maintain that, just like any other discipline, psychoanalysis can learn from what happens in the neighbouring fields.

1286

responseSet.title: The Couch and the Chador
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Couch and the Chador

Siamak Movahedi and Gohar Homayounpour

Gohar Homayounpour

252 Waban Ave, Waban, MA 02468, USA - siamak.movahedi@umb.edu

31 Roodsar Street, Tehran 15936, Iran - g.homayounpour@gmail.com

Gohar Homayounpour  

chador, fashion, second skin, maternal superego

(Final version accepte 12 July 2012)

The authors present a clinical discussion of the psychic functions of the chador, a veil-like outer garment worn in public by some Iranian women. Drawing on Anzieu's theoretical concept of the skin ego, the authors suggest that the chador does not just cover the body; it may also envelop the psyche and function as a second skin for the ego. The maternal function of a holding environment is symbolically displaced on any clothing that ‘hides,’ ‘covers,’ ‘veils,’ or ‘dresses’ the body. The skin's containing functions are extended through sensory and metonymic mediations to clothes, thus providing an imaginary maternal sack for the person. The clinical vignettes presented here suggest that for some Iranian women the chador may work, on one hand, as a second skin ego and a shield against a perceived intrusive world. On the other hand, it may work as a punitive maternal superego, a ‘holding cell’ in a jail that paralyses the separation/individuation process. Operating in the service of the patient's resistance and defense, the chador may also function as a psychic refuge and a place to hide on the couch.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parenting Imperfection: Unlocking Meaning in the Developing Relationship between a Father and His Disabled Son, in Gianni Amelio's The Keys to the House

Cheryl Moskowitz

Cheryl Moskowitz

1 Winton Avenue, London, N11 2AS - cmoskowitz@blueyonder.co.uk

Cheryl Moskowitz  

Departures, arrivals, connections. Trains and train stations feature significantly throughout Gianni Amelio's affecting film, The Keys to the House [Le chiavi di casa, 2004], in which young father, Gianni (Kim Rossi Stuart), meets 15 year-old Paolo (Andrea Rossi), the disabled son he abandoned at birth when the boy's mother died. It may be no accident that the father in the film shares his name, Gianni, with writer and director Gianni Amelio who fathered the film. And it is a satisfying coincidence that ‘Rossi’ occurs in the name of both the main actors playing the estranged father and son. This is a film about bonding and making connections but never glibly so. Amelio forces his characters and the viewers of the film to recognize the impossibility of being a perfect parent, especially when faced with the task of parenting a child with a disability which has labelled them, from the start, ‘imperfect’. Amelio composes many of his shots obliquely which work to accentuate a sense of uncertainty in the viewer. Through Gianni and Paolo's story we are invited, uncomfortably at times, to face our own weaknesses and fears, and to consider the question of responsibility that lies at the heart of every relationship between parent and child.

In the opening scene we see Alberto (Pierfrancesco Faviono) at a bar in an Italian train station drinking coffee. With him is Gianni. The two men sit for a while in awkward silence as the muted sounds of the station noises and conversations go on around them. Alberto, who we learn is Paolo's uncle, has brought the boy as far as Milan where Gianni is waiting and will meet his teenage son for the first time.

“How old was he here?” Gianni asks, looking at a photograph while the train, with the real boy asleep on it, is idling in the station. “Does he look like me?” Gianni is looking for something to ameliorate his feelings of guilt, some way of connecting in advance of the meeting which he is both longing for and dreading. “No,” says the brother-in-law flatly, puncturing that particular fantasy, “he's just like his mother”.

responseSet.title: On: On Neuropsychoanalytic Metaphysics (2011)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: On Neuropsychoanalytic Metaphysics (2011)

Brian Johnson MD

Brian Johnson

USA
E-mail: johnsonb@upstate.edu

Brian Johnson  

Dear Editor,

I was surprised to read in the IJPA that the theoretical base of neuropsychoanalysis, dual-aspect monism, is incoherent (Talvitie and Ihanus, 2011, p. 1597) or that “discussions on the subject of neuropsychoanalysis often wander aimlessly, devoid of clear points and arguments” (p. 1598). As the authors suggest, Occam's razor is an excellent tool for addressing issues and is indeed what I used in my article, Just what lies beyond the pleasure principle?, which showed the use of neuroscience tools to address metapsychological concepts of drive, cathexis (supported), repetition compulsion and death instinct (shown by Occam's razor to be explained more simply by earlier Freudian concepts) (Johnson, 2008). How to use neuropsychoanalytic interpretations was discussed in my article on cocaine addiction (Johnson, 2009). Without neuroscience interpretations, it would be difficult to operate on my medical school-based neuropsychoanalytic addiction service that had 2570 outpatient encounters in 2011. Drug dreams: A neuropsychoanalytic hypothesis (Johnson, 2001) also offers strong evidence that dreams are exactly an expression of wish-fulfillment - in contrast with Talvite and Ihanus' assertion that neuropsychoanalysis could not contribute on this issue (p. 1597).

Solms's (2000) dreaming pathway, established by his painstaking empirical research, solidly supports this psychoanalytic concept. His work, and mine, should be examples of the success of the dual-aspect monism approach.

1041

responseSet.title: Some Reflections on the Unique Time of Nachträglichkeit in Theory and Clinical Practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Reflections on the Unique Time of Nachträglichkeit in Theory and Clinical Practice

Paola Marion

Paola Marion

Via c. Monteverdi 15, 00198 Roma, Italy - p.marion@iol.it

Paola Marion  

Nachträglichkeit, trauma, sexuality, Oedipus, transference

(Final version accepted 9 September 2011)

Of the various forms that the matter of time assumes in analysis, Nachträglichkeit represents Freud's first intuition on the subject. The focus of this article is directed toward the specific temporal dimension that the concept of Nachträglichkeit expresses, and how that dimension, which overturns linear time, is expressed in clinical work. The concept of Nachträglichkeit is approached from a theoretical point of view, tracing back the role and development that this notion has had in psychoanalytic Freudian and post-Freudian thinking. The goal of this article is to demonstrate how Nachträglichkeit represents the unique temporal movement of the analytic session and the characteristic positioning of the mind of the analyst at work. Three clinical examples are presented. The analytic scene is formulated as occurring in two times, and through the working through that takes place, patients can recover the enigmatic ‘remainder’, which is consequently traumatic and which has compulsively accompanied them through the various times of their existence. Nachträglichkeit, as a non-linear temporality, introduces a unique dimension into the clinical work that influences listening to and interpretation of the material. The recognition of that (trauma, infantile sexuality, non-linear temporality) has consequences for the analyst's way of working in session and on the interpretation of clinical material, as I will try to show through my theoretical exposition and clinical examples.

responseSet.title: Clinical Issues in Analyses over the Telephone and the Internet
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Issues in Analyses over the Telephone and the Internet

Jill Scharff

Jill Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive, Chevy Chase, MD 20815-6504, USA - jillscharff@theipi.org

Jill Scharff  

analytic process, telephone/VOIP analysis, psychoanalytic training, distance learning

(Final version accepted 9 March 2011)

There is professional consensus that teleanalysis, the practice of psychoanalysis conducted remotely using the telephone and the Internet, is increasing in response to more mobility in the population. But there is controversy as to whether the use of technology leads to a dilution of analysis or to adaptive innovation that is clinically effective and true to the tenets of psychoanalysis. The author reviews the psychoanalytic literature and shows the development of analytic thinking about this technology-assisted practice of psychoanalysis. She summarizes analysts' perceptions and experiences of the advantages and disadvantages, and considers the indications and contra-indications. She focuses on the clinical concerns that arise in terms of the frame, resistance, and the development of analytic process through the unconscious communication of internal objects, unconscious fantasy, transference and countertransference. She gives vignettes from the analysis of a man with trauma-related depression to address the concerns raised and to support her argument that analysis using the telephone and the Internet is a viable, clinically effective alternative to traditional analysis where necessary.

responseSet.title: Identity and Inability to Mourn in The Skin I Live in
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identity and Inability to Mourn in The Skin I Live in

Domenico Di Ceglie

Domenico Di Ceglie

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA - ddiceglie@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Domenico Di Ceglie  

Pedro Almodóvar's film The Skin I Live In gives an interesting insight into the links between the vicissitudes of identity, some personality traits and

1308

their relationship to loss and trauma, among a number of other themes. In this review I will focus on these particular aspects as they are interesting from a psychological and psychoanalytical point of view.

The story is told largely in reverse, starting from the present and introducing flashbacks which provide a link between the current situation and past events. It is only near the end that the plot becomes evident to the audience, like in a thriller. I will mention here the key plot points relevant to the themes of my review.

The wife of Dr Ledgard, a leading plastic surgeon, has been involved in a car crash which has left her horribly burned. Following the accident, Dr Ledgard tries to save his wife who eventually kills herself when she becomes aware of her terrible appearance. At the beginning of the film, we see Dr Ledgard involved in a piece of research to create a pain-resistant skin which could be used to give his wife a new skin and to repair the extensive damage she had suffered. He is warned by a colleague about the professional risks of conducting an unethical piece of research, but this does not deter him from pursuing it at all costs. Later on we discover that his daughter, who has had psychiatric problems and is taking a number of psychotropic medications, is raped at a party by a young man. Dr Ledgard is bent on revenge. He kidnaps and imprisons this young man who eventually ends up in Dr Ledgard's home, which is also his private clinic and laboratory. After a number of twists in the story, we discover that Dr Ledgard has changed the sex of this young man. He was in fact the beautiful woman portrayed as Dr Ledgard's patient at the beginning of the film. We learn from a comment made by the mother of Dr Ledgard that this woman's appearance is identical to that of his dead wife. Dr Ledgard and his mother are finally killed by the woman.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Consenting to Be Robbed So as Not to Be Murdered’: Psychoanalytic Treatments of Chronically Depressed Patients in Two Parallel Depression Research Studies1414

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber  

In spite of the fact that it was taking place on Thursday evening (7.30 p.m. to 9.30 p.m.), the panel was well attended. In his introduction, Juan Pablo Jimenez (Santiago de Chile) indicated the societal context of the topic:

Depressive disorders constitute one of the main health problems worldwide. Recent studies confirm the impression that the clinical presentation of depression is much more varied than it is typically thought to be. Nowadays we know that subclinical conditions are very common and often lead to major depressive episodes; chronicity is more prevalent and subclinical symptoms can lead to serious disability. Longitudinal studies have shown that the various subtypes of depression in official diagnostic classifications (DSM-IV, ICD-10), such as minor depression and dysthymia, do not constitute discrete clinical disorders but are probably different phases of a single condition, expressed along a dimensional continuum of symptomatic severity (Judd, 2001; Merikangas et al, 2003). This view offered by modern psychiatry is closer to psychoanalysis, which has always stated that depression is better understood as a psychopathological dimension, ranging from the personality trait to the state or condition, than as a discrete diagnostic category.

14Moderator: Juan Pablo Jiménez. Panellists: Peter Fonagy (London, UK); David Taylor (London, UK); Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Tamara Fischmann, Nicole Pfenning, Alexa NegeJe (Frankfurt, Germany).751

responseSet.title: The Screen Memory and the Act of Remembering
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Screen Memory and the Act of Remembering

Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge

239 Central Park West, Ste. 1-BE, New York, NY 10024, USA - llafarge@verizon.net

Lucy LaFarge  

fantasy, Freud, Greenacre, memory, reconstruction, remembering, screen memory, trauma

(Final version accepted 21 November 2011)

Screen memories, seen by Freud, Greenacre and other analysts of a past generation as a key source of data for the reconstruction of psychic and historical reality, have been relatively neglected in contemporary analysis. A fresh look shows that these durable, constant memories have a dual relation to childhood experience: they memorialize both a specific organization of trauma, wish and defense, and a private childhood act of remembering. Close attention to the screen memory itself and the context in which it appears indicates that both aspects of screen memory have meaning for the individual and are represented in fantasy. Both currents of meaning can be seen in a literary screen memory and in the clinical situation, where both play out in transference and countertransference.

responseSet.title: Will Psychoanalysis Fulfill its Promise?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Will Psychoanalysis Fulfill its Promise?

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road, Belvedere, CA 94920, USA - judywall@comcast.net

Robert Wallerstein  

autonomous institute within the university, independent psychoanalytic institute, department-of-psychiatry-affiliated institute, psychoanalytic research

(Final version accepted 27 October 2011)

Although Freud had aspirations of a university structure for psychoanalytic education the sociopolitical structure of the Austro-Hungarian empire precluded this, and psychoanalysis developed by default in the central European heartland within a part-time, private-practice educational structure. With its rapid spread in the post- World- War-II United States, and its ready penetration of American academic psychiatry, a counter educational structure arose in some quarters: the department-of-psychiatry-affiliated institute within the medical school. This article outlines beyond these other, more ambitious, academic vistas (the David Shakow model, the Anna Freud model, the Menninger Foundation, Emory University (USA), AP de BA (Argentina)); conceptions even closer to the ideal (idealized) goal of full-time placement within the university, with strong links to medicine, to the behavioral sciences and to the humanities. The putative advantages of such a structure are presented.

responseSet.title: Intersubjectivity and Dialecticism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity and Dialecticism

Henry Schwartz

Henry Schwartz

41 Union Square West, Rm. 402, New York, NY 10003, USA - hps3@verizon.net

Henry Schwartz  

(Final version accepted 8 November 2011)

To restore silence is the role of objects.

(Samuel Beckett, Molloy, p. 13)

Introduction

There has been a psychoanalytic civil war being waged in the USA over the last 30 years between what has represented the mainstream tradition of American psychoanalysis, ego psychology, and an upstart that goes by a variety of names but which I will label here intersubjectivity. It may appear at this point that we should declare intersubjectivity the winner. Certainly the range of theorists writing with this outlook have attracted a wide following, and much that is written in contemporary ego psychology can be understood as a response to the various challenges posed by these revisionists. Yet, as André Green (2000) observed, intersubjectivity must be seen as more of a corrective movement than an outright revision of psychoanalysis. Psychoanalysis is and has always been intersubjective. ‘Intersubjectivity’, as a group of contemporary theories, addresses issues that have been present in our theory since Freud, but were inadequately theorized. The division between drive and object, internal and external, has been problematic throughout our history. Green believes that Freud emphasized drive and internality to the detriment of the object in order to focus attention on what was new in his theory. Beginning with Klein, Fairbairn and Winnicott, many theorists have sought to rectify that situation. Over the last 30 years we have seen that process intensify as intersubjective theories have come onto the stage and, at times, pushed drive and the intrapsychic to the wings. Any theory that eliminates one side of the intrapsychic-intersubjective, drive-object axes creates particular difficulties if it still wishes to be considered psychoanalytic. In their effort to provide the corrective psychoanalysis has long needed, these theorists call attention to the object side, but some also call for a dialectic between the two sides. Critics tend to omit this dialecticism from their remarks and present intersubjectivity as destructive of the concept of drive. Ultimately, it is not in terms of metapsychology that these writers are most innovative or have had the greatest impact, but rather in terms of clinical technique.

responseSet.title: The Therapeutic Action of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Action of Psychoanalysis

B. Brusset

B. Brusset

40 avenue d'Italie 75013 Paris - bernard.brusset@wanadoo.fr

B. Brusset  

actual psychoanalysis, involvement, psychotherapy, regression, transference, transformation psychique

(Final version accepted 15 September 2011)

Psychoanalysis does not seek to get rid of symptoms but to question them as witnesses of psychic functioning and as formations of the unconscious. Whatever their nature may be, it is a question of analysing their causes and their functions as they appear and develop during the course of the analytic process. The latter is activated by the transference relationship induced by the method within a specific setting. The aim is to bring about liberating psychic transformations. The extension of the indications and modifications in the expression of psychic suffering have led to the development of psychotherapies. Their relations with psychoanalysis proper have been evolving constantly since the first advances by Ferenczi. This long historical evolution has resulted in their redefinition. Psychoanalytic practices are currently considered to require, depending on the case, different settings and different modes of psychic involvement from the analyst. Contemporary psychoanalysis places emphasis on the internal setting of the analyst (thus his training), analysis of the countertransference, and the risk of anti-analytic aberrations.

responseSet.title: Kleinian Theory
responseSet.newSectionName: Reports of Panels Held at the 47th Congress of the International Psychoanalytic Association, Mexico City, 3-6 August 2011
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kleinian Theory88

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood  

This panel set out to discuss what has happened to Kleinian ideas and the Klein group in recent years. Albert Mason of the Psychoanalytic Center of California opened the panel with a detailed and systematic account of the cohorts of psychoanalysts and their contributions in the generations after 1960. The concepts originated by Klein have been expanded in certain respects but not replaced. The basic tenets of (a) the origins and initial harshness of the super-ego, (b) object relations from birth or before, and (c) projection, introjection and identification as the fundamental processes of development, all remain. They have, however, developed in themselves in the course of application to psychotic patients, with the energetic investigation of the paranoid—schizoid position by Herbert Rosenfeld, Hanna Segal and Wilfred Bion, and also in the application to understanding symbols and aesthetics (Segal), the nature of thinking (Bion), and to social studies (Elliott Jaques). Mason also included the development of the work of Kleinians in Los Angeles, following Bion's decade of residence there, notably James Grotstein's move towards intersubjectivity, Judith Mitrani's work on autistic states in adults as well as children, and Albert Mason's work on folie à deux.

He then listed the distinctive features of Kleinian thought and practice as they appear today:

The emphasis on the here-and-now

A stress in interpretations on functions rather than parts (particularly bodily ones)

The relationship between the paranoid—schizoid and depressive position states of mind

The constant fluctuation of those states analytically from session to session (even within a session)

The occurrence of paranoid—schizoid anxiety and defences as a fluctuating normal aspect of mature development

Rosenfeld's understanding of the psychotic's ‘living inside the object’

responseSet.title: On Procreating Today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Procreating Today

Giovanna Regazzoni

Giovanna Regazzoni

Via Panama 114, 00198 Rome, Italy - goretti.regazzoni@libero.it

Giovanna Regazzoni  

assisted conception, fertility, fertility dysfunctions, infertility, oneiric elaboration

(Final version accepted 12 April 2012)

This work reflects on the suffering and difficulties that men and women often experience during the process of procreation, beginning with the decision to suspend the use of contraception. The shared project that a child represents involves difficulties that are also a consequence of the culture in which we live: subject to early tests, ultrasound scans and so on, a planned pregnancy is dominated, throughout its development, by the secondary process of monitoring. A delay in conception, at times deriving from intolerance towards a body that does not immediately fulfil one's own desires, sets in motion the diagnostic inquiry. During this time, while the long-awaited moment of conception is further delayed, the desire for a son/daughter changes in quality and intensity. In a substantial number of cases, infertility remains unexplained, a situation in turn characterized by the fear that not knowing implies. Although the excessively simplistic and limited notion of psychogenic infertility has faded, a residue of unexpressed horror, difficult to frame even in analysis, persists in the couple. The end of the diagnostic inquiry means that assisted conception must be eventually faced. Fortunately, nowadays in psychoanalytic culture the fundamentalist tendencies that, in the past, led to forecasts of catastrophe, have subsided. Yet this remains a highly problematic area with many questions and few answers.

responseSet.title: Letter from Basle
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Basle

D. Bürgin

D. Bürgin

Psychoanalytiker SGPsa/IPA, CH, 4053, Basel
E-mail: dieter.buergin@unibas.ch

D. Bürgin  

Socio-Cultural Background

Switzerland resembles an enlarged city of eight million people, with a wonderful landscape of large, beautiful parks, mountains and lakes; there are five big agglomerations with universities. Geneva and Basle are located on its periphery opening towards other countries (France and France/Germany). Geneva's features are determined by the United Nations, Basle's by its chemical industry. Zurich functions as a cultural and commercial metropolis. Berne provides a kind of centre with the federal government there. Lausanne could possibly be called the provincial twin sister of Geneva. Switzerland's existence is not geographically, ethnically, or culturally determined and secured. A latent sense of threat is combined with a reactive guardedness. The boundaries of Switzerland and its neutrality have been dictated from outside by the Vienna Congress in 1815. Neutrality was a condition to prevent the passage through the Alps being dominated by one party. Viewed from the outside, Switzerland thus represents a kind of “road watch” on a major north-south transit through the Alps. In the idealized self-understanding of the Swiss, however, the Alps are often viewed more like their very own bastion. Switzerland engages in a skilful handling of minorities and offers them good protection. Its culture of compromise is exceptionally helpful but also slows down a lot of (progressive) steps. Big conflicts tend to be transformed into small and predictable ones. As a result many latent problems only become manifest after some time.

The proud myth of Switzerland as independent, autonomous, completely free and unconnected is linked to the knowledge that this is true for only a very small area. “The home of Switzerland is Europe”, says Peter von Matt, a prominent Swiss historian and literary scholar. This, however, refers to a Europe which, until the end of World War II, had been an agglomeration of antagonistic national states; presently, Europe is in the process of a massive transformation. Just as Europe is currently reflecting upon itself, Switzerland is also involved in an intense process of self-reflection geared towards a revised self-understanding, with the aim of an updated new identity.

responseSet.title: An Event in Medical Life, or the Advent of a Belief?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Event in Medical Life, or the Advent of a Belief?

Pascal-Henri Keller and Dolores Albarracin

Dolores Albarracin

EA4050 Département de Psychologie, Université de Poitiers, 97 avenue du Recteur Pineau, 86022 Poitiers Cedex, France - keller.pascal-henri@wanadoo.fr

Dolores Albarracin  

autopsy, Freud, slip, medicine, psychoanalysis

(Final version accepted 23 November 2011)

Freud's ambivalent relations with medicine are well known. Based on his relatively unknown text, A religious experience, published in 1928, the authors present an analysis of the internal reasons for this conflict. Responding to a letter from an American doctor, who tells him about his reactions to an autopsy and the influence it had on his religious faith, Freud suggests an analysis that leads him to make a surprising slip. This slip reveals the conflicts inherent in the practice of autopsy, considered as a peculiar event in medical training that puts a strain on the future medical practitioner's subjectivity. Based on the analysis of unconscious impulses and fantasmatic activity, the authors demonstrate that Freud's theory is still surprisingly relevant today in the light of testimonies from contemporary doctors confronted with autopsy during their training or daily practice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Coma: Man Deprived of His Consciousness

Xanthoula Dalakaki and Gregory Mantzouranis

Gregory Mantzouranis

14 rue Albert-Gos, 1206 Geneva, Switzerland - xdalakaki@bluemail.ch

1015 Lausanne, Switzerland - gregory.mantzouranis@psychologie.ch

Gregory Mantzouranis  

comatose condition, regression, splitting, trauma, perception of pain, denial

(Final version accepted 10 May 2012)

In this paper the authors reflect on the phenomena produced by the surprising communication between the unconscious of a therapist and that of a comatose patient. In a particularly disturbing context, when the patient's sternum is open and exposed, the therapist communicates empathically with the comatose person, identifying the patient's inexpressible experience and generating signs of a response from him in the form of the blinking of his eyelids. The absence of any reaction to pain, a pathognomonic sign of the comatose condition, could be related to the splitting-off of the trauma, as if the situation were frozen, through a denial of the sensory and neurological perception of pain - a kind of self-anaesthesia as a defence against the catastrophic anxieties raised by the threat of the return of the primary trauma. Starting from this encounter a relationship is formed whose guiding thread emerges in the shared illusion of a regression that makes it possible. It involves a process of returning to the past and a re-actualization of the past that includes the question of its change through representation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Crisis in Psychoanalysis: In Search of a Lost Doctrine by Ahmed FayekBridgeway Books, Austin, TX, 2009; 282 pp; $49.95

Christiane Gariepy-Boutin

Christiane Gariepy-Boutin

4139 Marcil, Montreal, Canada
e-mail: c.gariepy.boutin@hotmail.com

Christiane Gariepy-Boutin  

When I was asked to give my opinion on Ahmed Fayek's book, The Crisis in Psychoanalysis, I was told that it would be interesting to hear the point of view of a person beginning her psychoanalytical career about the work of a seasoned analyst at the end of his career. Ahmed Fayek has built up an impressive experience as an analyst, an educator, a hospital psychologist and a university teacher. Given this context, you may understand the great degree of humility with which I shall give my views on the contents of this book, the purpose of which is to return Freud's theories to their rightful rank.

Ahmed Fayek's plan is to prove that Freud's theories contain all the elements necessary to the practice of psychoanalysis, and that it is quite unnecessary to appeal to other theoretical perspectives in order to obtain a complete picture of the human psyche. Furthermore, in his view, the use of a plurality of theories and the existence of several schools of thought threaten the coherence and the very existence of psychoanalysis.

The author fears that psychoanalysis may collapse under the chaos of divergent, sometimes contradictory approaches, none of which, he argues, is capable of presenting a complete and general metapsychology comparable to Freud's. According to him, the lack of interest in psychoanalysis and the crisis which has enveloped psychoanalysis for many years now are caused by the loss of perception of Freudian doctrine as a coherent, intact thought system, no aspect of which can be changed without destroying the whole.

Fayek accuses those who criticize Freudian doctrine of having mainly attacked his metapsychology, the notion of the intrapsychic and psychodynamics as the weak points of his theory, points which need to be reformulated or could be eliminated without much impact on Freudian theory. According to him, these elements are now considered outdated and useless and this point of view is established and indisputable.

I have to say that I was very surprised when reading this part of Ahmed Fayek's book. I asked myself whether the psychoanalytic world in a French-speaking society could be so very different from that of an English-speaking society, both of which live side by side in the same city: Montreal.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Sexualidad en el Análisis: Escucha y Enactment [Sexuality in Analysis: Analytic Listening and Enactment]1515

Laura-Viviana Strauss

Laura-Viviana Strauss

Laura-Viviana Strauss  

This Spanish-speaking panel aimed to explore an apparent dichotomy. In managing different expressions of a patient's sexuality, the analyst either might be aware of its vicissitudes in order to interpret it or he might find himself involved in an enactment with the patient. In this latter case, the analyst shares an unconscious phantasy of which he is unaware because of his own defensive needs (Steiner, 1993) and thus is unable to interpret. Can this dichotomy be sustained? This panel raised significant questions: are enactment and interpretation opposing ways of exerting the analytic function? Is enactment an obstacle to listening or is it part of the analyst's listening function? Does enactment reveal a deadly compulsive repetition or does it aim to repair what the patient's psyche cannot repair?

In her poignant presentation of a clinical case of a patient she had terminated several years ago, Ana-Maria Rizzuto focused on the importance of listening both to the verbal manifestations of sexuality of the adult patient and to the infantile expressions of psychosexuality. She lay on the line how, during the first two years, his intense sarcasm and the constant rejection of anything the analyst said led her to conclude that his narcissism could not tolerate interpretation; she opted for clarifications and other milder interventions. Having first seen her patient as a ‘depressed child’ she decided to maintain a containing exploratory function, in this way transmitting her will to keep working with him. Inspired by a dream, she gradually discovered a fantasy of the patient: his belief that the only possibility of feeling loved by mother was “having sex with her”. Her enactment consisted in reviving his childhood mother in her countertransference, and attending to the child in him, while neglecting the adult. She then asked him to look at his fantasized experience of having actually raped the analyst through his expressions of sexuality, and find how it felt for him as an experiencing self. She was talking to him now as an experiencing person/self. When she said: “Now that you have raped me”, he and the analyst clearly knew that she was referring to a fantasized action. Rizzuto emphasized the importance of being acknowledged as a sexual object for the development of psychic life.

[[RunningHead]]

Review of the APA Journal: Revista de Psicoanálisis De la Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina

Susana Fischbein

Susana Fischbein

vinfisch@arnet.com.ar, susyvinfisch@gmail.com

Susana Fischbein  

Introduction

In response to the need to disseminate the psychoanalytic thought of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association after it was founded in December 1942, the first issue of the Revista de Psicoanálisis appeared in July 1943. It has become the most time-honoured continuous publication in Spanish. Its editorial guiding principles have always been to publicize the scientific work of the Association's members without neglecting the publication of relevant texts by authors all over the world. Freud's Analysis terminable and interminable was first published in Spanish in the Revista in 1946, and the Revista also provided access through early translations to the most prominent English and French authors (such as Klein, Winnicott, Bion, Lacan, Laplanche and Leclaire). Alongside current trends in Argentinean psychoanalysis, its volumes have addressed significant controversial topics in psychoanalysis over a long period, without neglecting the social environment, its diversity and the help it demands. From its inception, the Revista's pluralistic orientation has allowed the dissemination of different theoretical frames of reference.

Its current objectives are the transmission and scientific development of psychoanalysis through the publication of papers on methodology, psychoanalytic theory, technique and clinical practice. The Revista also publishes writings on the history of psychoanalysis, interdisciplinary studies, psychoanalytic contributions to mental health and culture, journals, book reviews and obituaries. Since 1977, it has not only been in charge of the official translation and publication of pre-published papers for the major panels of the IPA Congress, but it has also been given preference in the publication of accepted individual papers in Spanish. The possibility of revisiting many original works from the past 70 years has made the Revista a documentary testimony to the growth of psychoanalysis worldwide.

For several decades now the Revista has been published four times a year and is included in several International Bibliographic Catalogues (LATIN-DEX, LILACS and PSICODOC). An updated subject and author index is available for all its volumes. It is a fully peer-reviewed journal and external readers are selected for their level of expertise in their specific subjects. Contributions to the Revista are predominantly original papers that mainly address the subject chosen for each issue. The tendency is to produce monographic issues that may include a few articles on other topics. Key papers are sometimes given a commentary by external colleagues.

responseSet.title: On: Reply to ‘Skype and Privacy’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Reply to ‘Skype and Privacy’

Jill Scharff MD

Jill Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive, Chevy Chase, MD 20815, USA
E-mail: jillscharff@theipj.org

Jill Scharff  

Dear Editor,

It is useful to have updated information on communications technology drawn from very recent sources and published in security studies literature that most analysts do not read. I accept Dr Churcher's correction that Skype is not as secure as some people have been led to think. Maheu (2012) shares Dr Churcher's views on Skype but for slightly different reasons. She states that, even though Skype offers encryption, it does not claim to be HI-PAA compliant and, being a social media platform, it is unsuitable for telemental health assessment and treatment. I also stand corrected that VTC refers not to video technology company but to video teleconferencing. In my article I recommended VTC (meaning a paid videoteleconferencing capability) as the most secure technology to support distance psychoanalysis. More specifically, what is best is VTC with a medical-grade platform that claims to be HIPAA compliant (Maheu, 2012).

Nevertheless, Skype is in use by psychotherapists and psychoanalysts who believe that encryption makes it secure. Neumann (2012) referring to a presentation by Snyder (2011), distinguishes three types of Skype: “Skype phone, Skype voice over Internet protocol (VOIP), and Skype computer-to-computer connections, of which only Skype computer-to-computer is secure” (Snyder, 2011). On the contrary, Dr Churcher, drawing from Dupasquier et al. (2011), points out that, even though Skype is “known for its strong security policy … it is not entirely secure”, and even though Skype uses encryption it cannot be regarded as secure because its “proprietary software cannot be independently evaluated.” Dr Churcher quotes a review of security studies that found instances of “eavesdropping, interception, and modification” during Skype (Keromytis, 2012, in press). It is not clear to me whether these studies were focused on Skype phone, Skype VoIP, or Skype computer-to-computer, or indeed if distinctions in their degrees of security are relevant to the discussion. In any case, his point is well taken that analysts who are asked to continue an established psychoanalysis across a distance using communications technology, of which Skype is the main example, cannot rely on its claims of security.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fantasized Recipient-Donor Relationships following Lung Transplantations: A Qualitative Case Analysis Based on Patient Narratives

Marius Neukom , Valentina Corti , Brigitte Boothe , Annette Boehler and Lutz Goetzmann

Lutz Goetzmann

Binzmühlestrasse 14/ 16, CH-8050 Zürich, Switzerland - m.neukom@psychologie.uzh.ch

Lutz Goetzmann  

fantasy formation, lung transplantation, narrative analysis, organ integration, recipient—donor relationship

(Final version accepted 19 April 2011)

The present study examines the relationships between lung transplant recipients and their unknown, deceased donors. Out of 20 semi-structured interviews, eight narratives, by three female and three male recipients respectively, were identified in which the figure of the donor played a role. These narratives were examined using JAKOB, a qualitative research tool that analyses relational configurations and diagnoses psychodynamic conflicts. Analysis revealed a broad range of varying themes and relationships with equally varying wish and fear themes. All the narrators dealt either explicitly or implicitly with whether and how they are connected to their donors. In five narratives, specific personality traits were attributed to the figure of the donor; in four narratives, latent feelings of guilt concerning the donor's death were expressed. Indeed, the figure of the donor was not always perceived as an independent person, separate from the narrator's self: in two cases, the donor appears as part of the recipient's self, while in another case, the donor is presented as a transitional object for the recipient. The findings of the narrative analysis are discussed within a theoretical model of psychical organ integration.

responseSet.title: Lo Spazio Dell'interpretazione [The Space of Interpretation] by Sarantis Thanopulos Borla, Roma, 2009; 288 pp; €30.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lo Spazio Dell'interpretazione [The Space of Interpretation] by Sarantis ThanopulosBorla, Roma, 2009; 288 pp; €30.11

Giovanna Goretti

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114, 00198 Roma, Italy
e-mail: goretti.regazzoni@libero.it

Giovanna Goretti  

This book, which is not a straightforwardly ‘easy’ read, is organized into six chapters focusing on one of the fundamental themes of psychoanalysis -interpretation. On the cover there is an image in bold colours. At a glance, it appears as though it is of a bunch of white, red and blue anemones, seen through an unfocused and perhaps moving lens. But this is a Kandinsky painting and the title of the picture is Black Strokes. Already, we find ourselves at the heart of the complex interpretative process, an act which is both wise and naive, finished and yet always open, thanks to a few ‘strokes’ which escape interpretation or impose themselves upon it, determining it, blocking it or re-launching it.

The first chapter opens unexpectedly with a retreat (or an expansion?) to autobiographical material with an intensely emotive tone. It is as though the author were going to outline the place, the places, the presences, the feelings and the experiences of life in which the patients’ communications find welcoming spaces, points of resonance, areas of quiet working-through. In this space which is geographical, temporal, historical, affective, nostalgic - a space in which questions hide that may lack answers - we encounter the author's two analysts and the strength of feeling he has for them; we are told of the author's experience of reading an Introduction to Psychoanalysis in Greek at the age of 20; and we encounter Greece: the colonels, the metaphysical oppression of the dictatorship and, of course, his mother, his family, and the struggles. There is a strong sense of the ‘exiled sailor’ who carries the harbour (and hence his home) inside himself, someone who is in touch with the kind of suffering which could kill him. This pain is evoked in the words of the author's first analyst who feels unable to return to Chile for fear of being unable to survive this emotion.

The second chapter, ‘Interpretation as Explanation of Meaning’, is difficult, almost severe in the demand it imposes upon the reader for sustained and constant attention. And it seems as though it will never end. To give a detailed account of it would require a whole review; in giving a summarized version of it there is not just the risk but the certainty of omitting important aspects, and hence of misrepresenting it substantially.

responseSet.title: On: Response to Moran Shoham
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Response to Moran Shoham

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

SSPsa-SGPsa, 217 rte.d Annecy
Croix-de-Rozon, CH 1257, Switzerland
e-mail: adela.abella@bluewin.ch

Adela Abella  

Dear Editors,

I am grateful to Moran Shoham for her careful reading of my paper and her perceptive comments. They allow me to clarify the point of view I took in my paper. Moran Shoham is right to call our attention to the deep gap between “the artist's comments … (and) the meaning of his work”, between an artist's “conscious intent and the end product” (2010, p. 1526-7). A work of art often oversteps its author's aims. Incidentally, this also happens to an analyst's interpretation.

This gap has been recognized of old by a number of artists. Thus, for Duchamp, the artist's role is only that of a “medium” who is denied “the state of consciousness on the aesthetic plane about what he is doing or why he is doing it”. This is the reason why Duchamp attributes to the spectator the important role of “deciphering and interpreting” the artistic proposition, a role in which the audience's contribution has “as much importance that the one who makes it” (Duchamp, 1975, quoted in Abella, 2007). The problem is how to tackle this often profound gap between the artist's conscious intentions and his actual productions.

Traditionally there are two main paradigms which allow a psychoanalytical approach to art and culture. Both of them closely follow the avenues opened by Freud a hundred years ago: we might call them the Leonardo and Gradiva paradigms. The first of these aims to link the work of culture to the supposed unconscious conflicts of its author. The second pursues the understanding of the work of art in itself, that is, the uncovering of the (universal) fantasies underlying a given piece of culture. Although these two avenues have proved their usefulness at various levels, it would be wise to remember Freud's warning: “Yet it is evident that there is much slippery ground in many of our applications from psychoanalysis to biography and literature” (Freud, 1908-39, p. 408). The “slippery ground” of what Freud labelled “psychoanalytical novels” is most often understood in terms of a risk of wild interpretations which would overlook “the profound transformations through which an impression in an artist's life has to pass before it is allowed to make its contribution to a work of art” (Freud, 1910, p. 107).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Experience in the Treatment of Sexual Difficulties: Strengths and Limitations of the Psychoanalytic Approach1818

Carolyn Feigelson

Carolyn Feigelson

Carolyn Feigelson  

Theodore Jacobs grounded his Introduction in Freud's early connection between the damming up of sexual libido and somatic symptoms. Later, anxiety and/or depressive affect would underlie inhibited sexual pleasure, as did incomplete resolution of the oedipal situation, castration threat and fear of superego retaliation. Most often, sexual problems exist alongside character difficulties, object relations, narcissistic disorders, aggressive vicissitudes, somatic symptoms and phobias. Rarely today is sexual phobia a chief complaint. The paradox: since psychoanalysis is associated with psychosexual development, it should be, yet often is not, the most effective treatment for sexual problems. Cognitive and behavioral approaches offer explicit techniques from therapists who claim a high degree of success, faster than psychoanalysis. For certain patients, however, more deeply rooted psychological issues are most responsive to a combination of psychoanalysis and formulaic tactics.

Barbara Stimmel began with the image of an existential disconnect felt by many patients between mind and body, a sense of ‘not me’ in the refusal of the sexual body to respond. Sex therapy - exercises, homework, journal-keeping - places attention on body parts and reactions. The laser beam aimed at the sexual functioning of the body eliminates scrutiny of the mind. However, the mind is always present.

18Moderator: Theodore Jacobs. Panellists: Brian Robertson (Canada); Barbara Stimmel (USA); David Scharff (USA).756

responseSet.title: Leo Rangell 1913-2011
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Leo Rangell 1913-2011

Warren Procci

Warren Procci

230 S. San rafael Avenue, Pasadena CA 91105
E-mail: wrprocci@sbcglobal.net

Warren Procci  

Leo Rangell had an exceptional life. He left us on 29 May 2011, after a very brief illness and just a few months shy of his 98th birthday. He had been quite healthy, self-sufficient, vigorous, intellectually vital and seeing patients until just days before his death. He was actively involved with his three surviving, and very loving children, Judy, Susan and Paul, and enjoyed his relationship with his life partner and fellow psychoanalyst, Beth Kalish, Ph.D. And of course he remained a major figure in the world of psychoanalysis.

Leo was in his 8th decade of academic productivity, continuing to write in the scientific and popular press and preparing for an immediate upcoming professional meeting. His legacy is one of remarkable accomplishment in many areas, with an oeuvre of over 450 publications, including nine books, with more in progress. His books and scientific papers covered almost the entire range of key psychoanalytic ideas. His primary concern was the importance of a composite, unitary theory.

Leo's origins were humble. He was born in a poor section of Brooklyn in 1913 to immigrant Russian and Polish parents. The major focus for his parents was on survival. English was not spoken in the home, Yiddish was. Leo described home life as offering little intellectual stimulation, which stands in stark contrast to his adult life where he had an unquenchable thirst for understanding our world. Leo initially attended New York City public schools, and graduated from the Boys' High School in Brooklyn, known more at that time for grit than for scholarship.

451

A Disturbance in the Field: Essays in Transference-Counter Transference Engagement by Steven H. CooperRoutledge, New York, 2010; 256 pp; £33.26

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960A Piedmont Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94705, USA
e-mail: mdwmd@comcast.net

Mitchell Wilson  

Steven Cooper is among our very finest contributors to the literature on clinical psychoanalysis. This book of essays, many of which were published previously in somewhat different form, continues the high standard he has set in his earlier work.

While filled with thoughtful ideas, case descriptions and creative syntheses of disparate theoretical concepts, what is most impressive about these papers is the open and non-proprietary sensibility that marks every page and every sentence. When Cooper writes about his ideas, he is quick to include other psychoanalytic thinkers whose writings have influenced him. He does not get hung up on terms and names and in this sense is a ‘lumper’, not a ‘splitter’. While open-minded, Cooper is also rigorous. He is tough when he has to be -in how he thinks, what he writes and how he participates in his own clinical work. His ideas are drawn from a focus on unconscious conflict and fantasy (conflict theory a la Brenner and neo-Kleinian concepts), set against the background of American relational psychoanalysis (interpersonal experience and interaction).

808

responseSet.title: The Analysis of Failure by Arnold Goldberg New York, NY, London: Routledge, 2011; 243 pp; $34.95
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analysis of Failure by Arnold Goldberg New York, NY, London: Routledge, 2011; 243 pp; $34.95

Robert Wallerstein MD

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road, Belvedere, CA 94920, USA
E-mail: judywall@comcast.net

Robert Wallerstein  

During the ebulliently optimistic heyday of the popularization and the spread of psychoanalytic treatment through the educated strata of American society in the 1950s and 1960s, the typical promise and expectation was of the thorough cure of one's mental and emotional distress. In Leo Stone's widely read 1954 paper on The widening scope of indications for psychoanalysis, he wryly noted that: “Hopeless or grave reality situations, lack of talent or ability (usually regarded as ‘inhibition’), lack of an adequate philosophy of life, and almost any chronic physical illness may be brought to psychoanalysis for cure” (Stone, 1954, p. 568).

What was noted far less frequently was the unsettling awareness that such success did not, in reality, crown our efforts so uniformly, that there was a dark underbelly of significant treatment failures, or, as Arnold Goldberg states in this new book, The Analysis of Failure, a softening of such unhappy awareness by talking of less conclusive failures - impasses or stalemates. One of the few early considerations of these unhappier outcomes within American ranks was a 1964 panel at the annual meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association on the ‘limitations of psychoanalysis’ (Gray, 1965). Although limitations are not quite the same as failures, I grappled with these same problems in my opening paper on the goals of psychoanalysis, goals against which its limitations would be measured by the other panel participants (Wallerstein, 1965).

Today this dynamic of a lack of awareness of failure has been refuted very substantially, and it is no surprise that Goldberg essays a forthright look at treatment failure, based, in this instance, on a seminar on failed cases that he chaired at the Institute for Psychoanalysis of Chicago. Goldberg starts by declaring failure to, of course, be an ambiguous and problematic concept. What, after all, indubitably constitutes failure? Here is where the declared locutions for pending failures can be called impasses and stalemates, implying a temporary difficulty that can be (should be) overcome - thus, not yet real failure.

responseSet.title: Ambiguity: by Simona Argentieri: Einaudi, Turin, 2008; 122 pp; €9.00
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ambiguity: by Simona Argentieri: Einaudi, Turin, 2008; 122 pp; €9.00

Adolfo Pazzagli

Adolfo Pazzagli

Via Daniele Manin, 4 - 50136 Firenze, Italy E-mail: apazzagli@tin.it

Adolfo Pazzagli  

Although the ancient Greeks said that large books can be a great evil, the contrary is not necessarily true. A small book can also be a great evil but, vice versa, it can also be of considerable importance. And this is the case of Ambiguity, a book that supplies continuous and valuable stimulation to the reader not only from the professional point of view, but also because of those personal, cognitive and affective aspects that inevitably form part of every psychoanalyst's activity but that normally remain private.

“We are born with an innate love of the truth, but we are ready to free ourselves of it as soon as it becomes a nuisance.” This striking sentence by a great psychoanalyst, Roger Money-Kyrle, that Simona Argentieri has chosen as her ‘exergo’, is, I think, perfect as an introduction to the theme. The author directs her attention to a particularly subtle and elusive mental attitude that is difficult to define. She first noticed it in certain of her patients, and then - increasingly - in everyday public and private relationships, to the extent that she considers it a widespread tendency of today's Western culture.

The book supplies us with information but at the same time it raises and clarifies problems and stimulates the reader's memories, personal experiences and fantasies. The literary style is clear and colloquial but also precise and intense; through it we can appreciate the author's profound psychoanalytic knowledge gathered not from book learning but from actual experiences both personal and with her patients. This stimulating book opens up new avenues of thought that are often unusual; they become wider and more enlightening with every reading and often provide new observation points on the theme under discussion.

Although Argentieri's book is apparently easy to read, it is at the same time original and profound; it can be understood immediately, but it certainly cannot be read distractedly or too rapidly or its fundamental capacity for stimulating thoughts, feelings, imagination and associations would be lost. To use the same word as the title, the book is not ‘ambiguous’ but it is non-saturated: it helps us to feel directly the complexity and the contradiction of the mental life of human beings. This is why it is not so much a book that imparts information, but rather one that presents problems and perspectives in an essentially instructive manner. In fact, ambiguity and bad faith are featuring more and more as dominant and invasive traits of our present age - in love and in social relationships, in politics and in bio-ethics - because they evade interpersonal and intrapsychic truth; they are at the same time a neurosis as well as a minor offence.

[[RunningHead]]

The European Annuals of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis: The Benefits of Linguistic Diversity

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

53A chemin des Fourches, 1223 Cologny, Geneva, Switzerland - quinodoz.jm@gmail.com

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

Annuals, International Journal of Psychoanalysis, translation, translators, terminology, multilingualism, lingua franca, mother tongue, deferred action

(Final version accepted 6 June 2011)

This paper presents the Annuals of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, which have been published in Europe since 2003, successively in French, Italian, German, Turkish and Russian. Like the Annuals published since 1985 in Latin America, these are designed to give non-Anglophone readers access to a selection of leading psychoanalytic articles. Each European Annual makes its own selection so the content varies from one Annual to another. Psychoanalysts on the various editorial committees translate the texts from English into their native target language on a voluntary basis. Beyond the geographical and cultural criteria, the desire to translate and the enjoyment of translating are among the key criteria. The Annual editors consider it important that the English language should continue to exist as a psychoanalytic lingua franca, without actually becoming its universal language, so that the richness and diversity of the psychoanalytic languages and terminologies can be preserved. The European Annuals in French, Italian, German, Turkish and Russian, with their linguistic and cultural characteristics, are then presented in turn.
